1.1: Many writers have set their hand to composing a narrative of the events that have unfolded among us,
1.2: following the testimony of those who were eyewitnesses from the start and who became servants of this message,
1.3: it has seemed right for me as well—having carefully traced everything from its origins—to set it out in sequence for you, honored Theophilus,
1.4: so that you may have full confidence in what you have been taught.
1.5: During the reign of Herod over Judea there was a priest named Zechariah who served in the division of Abijah. His wife Elizabeth was of priestly lineage—a descendant of Aaron.
1.6: Both were righteous before God, living blamelessly in every commandment and requirement the Lord had given.
1.7: They had no children, for Elizabeth was barren, and both were well past the age of having a family.
1.8: One day while his priestly division was serving its rotation before God,
1.9: he was chosen by lot—as the priestly custom required—to enter the Lord's sanctuary and offer incense.
1.10: The whole assembly of worshippers was gathered outside, praying, at the time of the incense offering.
1.11: Suddenly an angel of the Lord appeared to him, standing at the right side of the incense altar.
1.12: The sight of the angel terrified Zechariah and he was seized with fear.
1.13: The angel said: Don't be afraid, Zechariah—God has heard your prayer. Your wife Elizabeth will conceive and bear a son, and you will name him John.
1.14: He will bring you great joy and happiness, and his birth will be a cause for rejoicing among many.
1.15: He will be great in the Lord's sight. He is to abstain from wine and strong drink entirely, and he will be filled with the Holy Spirit from before his birth.
1.16: He will turn many in Israel back to the Lord their God.
1.17: He will go ahead of the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah, turning the hearts of parents toward their children and the rebellious toward the wisdom of the righteous—preparing a people ready to receive the Lord.
1.18: Zechariah said to the angel: How can I know this is real? I'm an old man, and my wife is well past childbearing age.
1.19: The angel replied: I am Gabriel—I stand in the very presence of God. He sent me to you to bring you this good news.
1.20: But because you did not believe my words—which will be fulfilled at the right time—you will be struck silent and unable to speak until the day this comes to pass.
1.21: Outside, the people were waiting for Zechariah, puzzled by how long he was taking in the sanctuary.
1.22: When he finally came out, he could not speak a word. They understood he had seen a vision in the sanctuary—he kept gesturing to them but stayed completely mute.
1.23: When his period of temple service ended, he went home.
1.24: Some time later his wife Elizabeth became pregnant and kept to herself for five months.
1.25: This is what the Lord has done for me, she said. He has looked on me with favour and removed the shame I bore among my people.
1.26: Six months into Elizabeth's pregnancy, God sent the angel Gabriel to a town in Galilee called Nazareth,
1.27: with a message for a young woman named Mary, who was pledged to marry Joseph, a descendant of David.
1.28: Gabriel came to her and said: Greetings, Mary—you are deeply favored by God! The Lord is with you.
1.29: Mary was deeply unsettled by these words and tried to make sense of what kind of greeting this could be.
1.30: The angel said: Don't be afraid, Mary—you have found extraordinary favor with God.
1.31: You will become pregnant and give birth to a son, and you are to name him Jesus.
1.32: He will be great—called the Son of the Most High. The Lord God will give him the throne of his ancestor David;
1.33: he will reign over the people of Jacob forever, and his kingdom will have no end.
1.34: Mary said to the angel: But how can this happen? I have never been with a man.
1.35: The angel replied: The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will cover you. Because of this, the holy child born to you will be called the Son of God.
1.36: And consider this: your relative Elizabeth has also conceived a son in her old age—she who was called barren is already six months along.
1.37: For nothing is impossible with God.
1.38: Mary said: I am the Lord's servant. Let it be exactly as you have said. And with that the angel left her.
1.39: In those days Mary set out quickly and traveled to a town in the hill country of Judea,
1.40: where she entered Zechariah's home and greeted Elizabeth.
1.41: The moment Elizabeth heard Mary's greeting, the baby inside her leaped for joy, and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit.
1.42: She cried out in a loud voice: You are the most blessed of all women, and blessed is the child you carry!
1.43: How has this honor come to me—that the mother of my Lord should visit me?
1.44: The moment your greeting reached my ears, the child inside me leaped with joy.
1.45: How blessed you are for believing that what the Lord promised you would actually come to pass!
1.46: And Mary broke into song: My whole being magnifies the Lord,
1.47: my spirit overflows with joy in God my Savior,
1.48: for he has turned his eye toward the lowliness of his servant—from now on every generation will call me blessed,
1.49: for the Mighty One has accomplished great things for me—holy is his name.
1.50: His mercy flows to all who hold him in reverence, from one generation to the next.
1.51: He has unleashed the power of his arm—scattering those whose hearts are swollen with pride.
1.52: He has toppled rulers from their thrones and raised up the humble in their place.
1.53: He has satisfied the hungry with good things and sent the wealthy away with nothing.
1.54: He has taken hold of his servant Israel, faithful to his covenant mercy,
1.55: just as he promised our ancestors—to Abraham and his descendants forever.
1.56: Mary stayed with Elizabeth about three months before returning to her own home.
1.57: When the time came, Elizabeth gave birth to a son.
1.58: Her neighbors and relatives heard how the Lord had shown his great mercy to her, and they celebrated with her.
1.59: On the eighth day they gathered to circumcise the child. They assumed he would be named Zechariah after his father—
1.60: but his mother spoke up firmly: No—he is to be called John.
1.61: But they objected: No one in your family has ever had that name.
1.62: They turned to Zechariah, gesturing to find out what name he wanted.
1.63: He asked for a writing tablet and wrote plainly: His name is John. Everyone was astonished.
1.64: At that instant his mouth opened, his tongue was freed, and he began to speak—praising God.
1.65: A sense of awe fell on all their neighbors, and news of these events spread through the entire hill country of Judea.
1.66: Everyone who heard about it took it to heart and wondered: What is this child going to become? For the Lord's hand was clearly upon him.
1.67: His father Zechariah was filled with the Holy Spirit and broke into prophetic song:
1.68: Praise the Lord God of Israel—he has come to his people and set them free!
1.69: He has raised up a mighty Savior for us from the royal line of his servant David—
1.70: just as he promised long ago through his holy prophets—
1.71: rescue from our enemies and deliverance from all who hate us,
1.72: to show the mercy he promised our ancestors, honoring his holy covenant,
1.73: the oath he swore to our ancestor Abraham—
1.74: to free us from our enemies so we could serve him without fear,
1.75: living in holiness and right relationship with him all our days.
1.76: And you, my child—you will be called a prophet of the Most High, for you will go ahead of the Lord to prepare his path,
1.77: bringing his people the knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of their sins—
1.78: all flowing from the tender mercy of our God, by which the dawn from on high will break upon us,
1.79: to shine on all who sit in darkness and death's shadow, and to guide our feet onto the path of peace.
1.80: The child grew strong in spirit and lived in the wilderness until the day he appeared publicly to Israel.

2.1: In those days Emperor Augustus issued an order for a census of the whole Roman world.
2.2: This was the first census carried out while Quirinius governed Syria.
2.3: Everyone traveled to their ancestral hometown to register.
2.4: Joseph therefore went up from Galilee—from Nazareth—to Judea, to the city of David called Bethlehem, because he belonged to the house and lineage of David.
2.5: He went to register along with Mary, to whom he was engaged—and she was expecting a child.
2.6: While they were in Bethlehem the time came for the baby to be born,
2.7: and she gave birth to her firstborn son. She wrapped him in strips of cloth and laid him in a feeding trough, because no lodging was available for them.
2.8: In the fields nearby, shepherds were keeping night watch over their flocks.
2.9: Suddenly an angel of the Lord appeared to them, and the radiance of the Lord blazed around them—they were overcome with terror.
2.10: But the angel said: Don't be afraid! I bring you wonderful news—great joy for all people.
2.11: Today in the city of David, a Savior has been born to you—he is the Messiah, the Lord.
2.12: Here is the sign to confirm it: you will find a newborn baby, wrapped in cloth and lying in a feeding trough.
2.13: At that moment a vast company of the heavenly army appeared with the angel, praising God:
2.14: Glory to God in the highest! And peace on earth to all those who carry his favor.
2.15: When the angels had returned to heaven, the shepherds said to each other: Let's go straight to Bethlehem and see this event that the Lord has made known to us.
2.16: They hurried off and found Mary and Joseph, and the baby lying in the feeding trough.
2.17: Once they had seen the child, they spread the news of what they had been told about him,
2.18: and everyone who heard it was amazed at what the shepherds told them.
2.19: But Mary quietly held all of these things close, turning them over in her heart.
2.20: The shepherds returned, glorifying and praising God for everything they had heard and seen—it was exactly as they had been told.
2.21: On the eighth day, when the time came for his circumcision, he was named Jesus—the name the angel had given before he was even conceived.
2.22: When the days required for purification under Moses' law were completed, they brought him up to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord,
2.23: as the Law of the Lord requires: Every firstborn male shall be dedicated to the Lord,
2.24: and to offer the sacrifice prescribed in the Lord's law: a pair of doves or two young pigeons.
2.25: At that time there was a man in Jerusalem named Simeon—righteous and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel—and the Holy Spirit rested on him.
2.26: The Holy Spirit had revealed to him that he would not die before he had seen the Lord's Messiah.
2.27: Led by the Spirit, he entered the temple courts. When the parents brought in the child Jesus to carry out what the law required,
2.28: Simeon took the child in his arms and praised God:
2.29: Lord, now you can let your servant depart in peace, just as you promised,
2.30: for my eyes have seen your salvation—
2.31: the deliverance you have made ready in full view of every nation:
2.32: a light to illuminate the nations, and the glory of your people Israel.
2.33: The child's father and mother were astonished at what was being said about him.
2.34: Then Simeon blessed them and said to Mary his mother: This child is destined to bring about the fall and rise of many in Israel, and to be a sign that people will oppose—
2.35: and a sword will pierce through your own soul as well—so that what lies hidden in many hearts may be laid bare.
2.36: There was also a prophetess named Anna, daughter of Phanuel, from the tribe of Asher. She was very old—having lived with her husband seven years after her marriage,
2.37: then a widow until she was eighty-four. She never left the temple but served God night and day with fasting and prayer.
2.38: Coming forward at that very moment, she gave thanks to God and spoke about the child to everyone who was waiting for Jerusalem's redemption.
2.39: When they had done everything required by the Lord's law, they returned to Galilee and their hometown of Nazareth.
2.40: The child grew and became strong, filled with wisdom, and the grace of God rested on him.
2.41: Every year his parents traveled to Jerusalem for the Passover festival.
2.42: When he was twelve, they made the journey as usual.
2.43: When the festival was over and his parents headed home, the boy Jesus stayed behind in Jerusalem without them knowing.
2.44: Assuming he was traveling with others in their group, they went a full day's journey before searching for him among relatives and friends.
2.45: When they could not find him, they turned back to Jerusalem to search.
2.46: Three days later they found him in the temple, sitting in the middle of the teachers, listening to them and asking questions.
2.47: Everyone who heard him was astonished at his understanding and the answers he gave.
2.48: When his parents saw him, they were overcome. His mother said: Son, why did you do this to us? Your father and I have been searching for you in anguish.
2.49: He replied: Why were you looking for me? Didn't you know I had to be in my Father's house?
2.50: But they did not grasp what he meant.
2.51: He went back to Nazareth with them and was obedient to them. His mother kept all these things stored in her heart.
2.52: And Jesus grew in wisdom and maturity, gaining favor with both God and people.

3.1: In the fifteenth year of Emperor Tiberius's reign—when Pontius Pilate governed Judea, Herod ruled Galilee, his brother Philip ruled Iturea and Trachonitis, and Lysanias ruled Abilene—
3.2: during the high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas—the word of God came to John, son of Zechariah, in the wilderness.
3.3: He went throughout the whole region around the Jordan, proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins.
3.4: This is what Isaiah the prophet wrote: A voice calling in the wilderness—Prepare the road for the Lord; make his paths straight!
3.5: Every valley will be filled in, every mountain and hill leveled; the winding roads will be straightened and the rough terrain made smooth.
3.6: And every person on earth will see God's salvation.
3.7: To the crowds coming out to be baptized, John said: You snake-brood! Who tipped you off to escape the coming judgment?
3.8: Show by the way you live that you have genuinely turned to God—and don't tell yourselves: We have Abraham as our ancestor. I'm warning you, God could create children for Abraham out of these stones.
3.9: The axe is already positioned at the base of the trees. Every tree that fails to produce good fruit will be chopped down and thrown into the fire.
3.10: The people asked him: Then what should we do?
3.11: John answered: If you have two shirts, give one to someone who has none. If you have food, share it with those who are hungry.
3.12: Even tax collectors came to be baptized and asked: Teacher, what should we do?
3.13: He told them: Collect only what the law requires—nothing more.
3.14: Some soldiers asked him the same question: And us—what should we do? He replied: No extortion, no false accusations—and be satisfied with your wages.
3.15: The people were filled with anticipation, and everyone was wondering in their hearts whether John might be the Messiah.
3.16: John answered everyone: I baptize you with water. But someone far more powerful than I is coming—I'm not even worthy to untie his sandals. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire.
3.17: He comes with his threshing fork ready—to separate wheat from chaff, gathering the grain into his storehouse and burning the chaff in an inextinguishable fire.
3.18: And with many such warnings and encouragements, he proclaimed the good news to the people.
3.19: But Herod the ruler, whom John had rebuked for marrying Herodias his brother's wife—and for all his other misdeeds—
3.20: topped everything by throwing John in prison.
3.21: When all the people had been baptized, Jesus was baptized as well. While he was praying, the sky opened—
3.22: and the Holy Spirit descended on him in a physical form like a dove. A voice came from heaven: You are my Son, the one I cherish—you bring me great joy.
3.23: Jesus was about thirty years old when he began his ministry. He was known as the son of Joseph, son of Heli,
3.24: son of Matthat, son of Levi, son of Melchi, son of Jannai, son of Joseph,
3.25: son of Mattathias, son of Amos, son of Nahum, son of Esli, son of Naggai,
3.26: son of Maath, son of Mattathias, son of Semein, son of Josech, son of Joda,
3.27: son of Joanan, son of Rhesa, son of Zerubbabel, son of Shealtiel, son of Neri,
3.28: son of Melchi, son of Addi, son of Cosam, son of Elmadam, son of Er,
3.29: son of Joshua, son of Eliezer, son of Jorim, son of Matthat, son of Levi,
3.30: son of Simeon, son of Judah, son of Joseph, son of Jonam, son of Eliakim,
3.31: son of Melea, son of Menna, son of Mattatha, son of Nathan, son of David,
3.32: son of Jesse, son of Obed, son of Boaz, son of Sala, son of Nahshon,
3.33: son of Amminadab, son of Ram, son of Hezron, son of Perez, son of Judah,
3.34: son of Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham, son of Terah, son of Nahor,
3.35: son of Serug, son of Reu, son of Peleg, son of Eber, son of Shelah,
3.36: son of Cainan, son of Arphaxad, son of Shem, son of Noah, son of Lamech,
3.37: son of Methuselah, son of Enoch, son of Jared, son of Mahalalel, son of Kenan,
3.38: son of Enosh, son of Seth, son of Adam—son of God.

4.1: Jesus returned from the Jordan filled with the Holy Spirit and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness,
4.2: where for forty days he was tested by the devil. He ate nothing during that time, and at the end he was famished.
4.3: The devil said to him: If you really are the Son of God, order this stone to turn into bread.
4.4: Jesus replied: Scripture says, Human beings do not live on bread alone.
4.5: The devil took him up and showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a single flash,
4.6: and said: I will give you all this power and glory—it has been handed to me and I can give it to anyone I choose.
4.7: All of it can be yours if you will bow down and worship me.
4.8: Jesus replied: Scripture says, Worship and serve the Lord your God alone.
4.9: The devil then led him to Jerusalem and placed him on the highest point of the temple and said: If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down from here—
4.10: because Scripture says: He will instruct his angels to watch over you,
4.11: and they will hold you up with their hands so you won't even stub your foot on a stone.
4.12: Jesus replied: But Scripture also says, Do not put God to a reckless test.
4.13: When the devil had exhausted every test, he left Jesus—until a more suitable opportunity.
4.14: Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit, and word about him spread throughout the whole surrounding region.
4.15: He taught in the synagogues and was praised by everyone.
4.16: He came to Nazareth, where he had grown up. On the Sabbath he went to the synagogue as was his habit and stood up to read.
4.17: The scroll of the prophet Isaiah was handed to him. He unrolled it and found the passage that reads:
4.18: The Spirit of the Lord is on me—he has anointed me to bring good news to the poor. He has sent me to announce freedom to captives, sight to the blind, to release those who are crushed,
4.19: to declare the year when the Lord shows his favor.
4.20: He rolled up the scroll, returned it to the attendant, and sat down. Every eye in the synagogue was fixed on him.
4.21: He began: Today, as you have heard it, this scripture has come true.
4.22: Everyone was speaking well of him, astonished at the words of grace that poured from his mouth. They asked: Isn't this Joseph's son?
4.23: He said: No doubt you'll throw this proverb at me: Doctor, heal yourself! Do here in your hometown what we've heard you did in Capernaum.
4.24: I tell you honestly: no prophet is welcomed in his own hometown.
4.25: Let me remind you: in Elijah's time there were many widows in Israel when no rain fell for three and a half years and famine gripped the land.
4.26: Yet Elijah was sent to none of them—only to a widow in Zarephath, in Sidon.
4.27: And in Elisha's time there were many with leprosy in Israel—yet not one of them was healed, only Naaman the Syrian.
4.28: When they heard this, everyone in the synagogue was filled with rage.
4.29: They rose up, expelled him from the town, and drove him to the cliff edge of the hill on which Nazareth stood, intending to throw him off.
4.30: But he walked straight through the crowd and went on his way.
4.31: He went down to Capernaum, a town in Galilee, and taught there on the Sabbath.
4.32: They were astonished at his teaching—he spoke with genuine authority.
4.33: In the synagogue there was a man with an unclean demonic spirit who shrieked at the top of his voice:
4.34: Leave us alone! What business do you have with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are—the Holy One of God!
4.35: Jesus rebuked it sharply: Be quiet! Come out of him! The demon hurled the man down in the middle of the crowd and came out without hurting him.
4.36: Everyone was stunned and kept saying to each other: What is this? He commands unclean spirits with real authority and power—and they obey!
4.37: And word about him spread to every corner of the surrounding region.
4.38: Jesus left the synagogue and went to Simon's home. Simon's mother-in-law was suffering from a severe fever, and they appealed to Jesus for her.
4.39: He stood over her, rebuked the fever, and it left her instantly. She got up at once and began serving them.
4.40: As the sun was setting, people brought to Jesus everyone who was sick with any kind of disease. He laid his hands on each one and healed them.
4.41: Demons came out of many people, shouting: You are the Son of God! But he rebuked them and refused to let them speak, because they knew he was the Messiah.
4.42: At daybreak Jesus went out to a deserted place. The crowds searched for him, and when they found him they tried to prevent him from leaving.
4.43: But he said: I must announce the good news of God's kingdom to the other towns as well—this is why I was sent.
4.44: And he went on preaching in the synagogues of Judea.

5.1: One day the crowd was pressing in around Jesus to hear God's word as he stood beside the Lake of Gennesaret.
5.2: He noticed two boats pulled up at the water's edge—the fishermen had left them and were washing their nets.
5.3: He got into Simon's boat and asked him to push out a little from shore. Then he sat down in the boat and taught the crowds.
5.4: When he had finished teaching, he said to Simon: Push out into deep water and let down your nets for a catch.
5.5: Simon replied: Master, we worked all night without catching a thing. But because you say so, I'll let down the nets.
5.6: When they did, they netted such an enormous haul of fish that their nets began to tear.
5.7: They waved to their partners in the other boat to come and help. They came, and both boats were so loaded they began to sink.
5.8: When Simon Peter saw this, he dropped to his knees before Jesus and said: Go away from me, Lord—I am a sinful man!
5.9: He and all his companions were awestruck at the catch they had taken,
5.10: including James and John, the sons of Zebedee, Simon's partners. Jesus said to Simon: Don't be afraid. From now on you'll be catching people.
5.11: They brought their boats to shore, abandoned everything, and followed him.
5.12: While Jesus was in one of the towns, a man covered with leprosy saw him and fell with his face to the ground, begging: Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean.
5.13: Jesus reached out and touched him. I am willing, he said. Be clean! Immediately the leprosy vanished.
5.14: Jesus instructed him: Tell no one. But go and present yourself to the priest and offer what Moses prescribed for your cleansing—as a witness to them.
5.15: Yet word about him spread even further, and large crowds gathered to hear him and to be healed of their sicknesses.
5.16: But Jesus would regularly withdraw to deserted places to pray.
5.17: One day as Jesus was teaching, Pharisees and teachers of the law were seated there—they had come from every town in Galilee and Judea and from Jerusalem. The power of the Lord was present to heal.
5.18: Some men arrived carrying a paralyzed man on a mat, trying to bring him in and lay him before Jesus.
5.19: Unable to get through because of the crowd, they went up on the roof and lowered the man on his mat down through the tiles, right in front of Jesus.
5.20: When Jesus saw their faith, he said: Friend, your sins are forgiven.
5.21: The Pharisees and teachers of the law started saying to each other: Who does this man think he is? This is blasphemy! Who can forgive sins except God alone?
5.22: Jesus knew what they were thinking and said: Why do you have such thoughts?
5.23: Which is easier to say: Your sins are forgiven—or: Get up and walk?
5.24: But so you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins— he turned to the paralyzed man and said: I tell you, get up, pick up your mat, and go home.
5.25: Instantly the man stood up before them all, picked up what he had been lying on, and went home praising God.
5.26: Amazement gripped everyone. They praised God and were filled with awe, saying: We have seen extraordinary things today.
5.27: After this Jesus went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at his collection booth. Jesus said: Follow me.
5.28: Levi got up, left everything behind, and followed him.
5.29: Then Levi threw a great banquet for Jesus at his home. A large crowd of tax collectors and others were reclining at the table with them.
5.30: The Pharisees and their religious scholars complained to his disciples: Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?
5.31: Jesus replied: Healthy people don't need a doctor—sick people do.
5.32: I came not to call the self-righteous, but to call sinners to turn back to God.
5.33: They said to him: John's disciples fast frequently and pray, and the Pharisees' disciples do the same—but your disciples just eat and drink.
5.34: Jesus replied: You can't ask the wedding party to fast while the bridegroom is right there with them, can you?
5.35: But the days are coming when the bridegroom will be taken away—and then they will fast.
5.36: He also gave them this parable: No one cuts a patch from a new garment to repair an old one—that would ruin the new garment, and the new patch wouldn't match the old anyway.
5.37: And no one pours new wine into old wineskins—the new wine will split the old skins, spilling the wine and ruining the skins.
5.38: New wine belongs in new wineskins.
5.39: And no one who has been drinking old wine wants the new—they say, The old is better.

6.1: One Sabbath Jesus was walking through the grain fields, and his disciples were picking heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands, and eating them.
6.2: Some Pharisees said: Why are you doing what is not permitted on the Sabbath?
6.3: Jesus replied: Haven't you read what David did when he and his men were hungry?
6.4: He went into God's house and took the consecrated bread—which only priests are allowed to eat—and ate it, and gave some to his companions.
6.5: Then he told them: The Son of Man is master even of the Sabbath.
6.6: On another Sabbath he entered the synagogue and was teaching. A man was there whose right hand was withered.
6.7: The religious scholars and Pharisees were watching closely to see if he would heal on the Sabbath, looking for grounds to accuse him.
6.8: But Jesus knew their thoughts and said to the man with the withered hand: Get up and stand out here. The man got up and stood there.
6.9: Jesus said to them: Let me ask you something: on the Sabbath, which is the right thing to do—good or evil? To save life or to destroy it?
6.10: He looked around at all of them and said to the man: Stretch out your hand. He did—and his hand was completely restored.
6.11: They were filled with rage and began discussing what they might do to Jesus.
6.12: In those days Jesus went out to the hill country to pray and spent the whole night in prayer to God.
6.13: When morning came, he called his disciples together and chose twelve of them, whom he also designated as apostles:
6.14: Simon, whom he named Peter, and his brother Andrew; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew;
6.15: Matthew and Thomas; James son of Alphaeus; Simon who was called the Zealot;
6.16: Judas son of James; and Judas Iscariot, who later betrayed him.
6.17: He came down with them and stood on a level place. A large crowd of his disciples was there, along with an enormous gathering from all over Judea, from Jerusalem, and from the coast of Tyre and Sidon.
6.18: They had come to hear him and to be healed of their diseases. Those tormented by unclean spirits were being freed,
6.19: and everyone in the crowd was trying to touch him—because power was flowing from him and healing them all.
6.20: Looking at his disciples, he said: You who are poor are favored by God—the kingdom of God belongs to you.
6.21: You who are hungry now are favored by God—you will be satisfied. You who weep now are favored—you will laugh.
6.22: You are favored by God when people hate you, shut you out, insult you, and reject your name as evil—because of the Son of Man.
6.23: Rejoice and leap for joy when that happens—your reward in heaven is enormous. For that is exactly how their ancestors treated the prophets.
6.24: But watch out, you who are wealthy—you have already received your comfort in full.
6.25: Watch out, you who are stuffed full now—you will go hungry. Watch out, you who are laughing now—you will mourn and weep.
6.26: Watch out when everyone speaks well of you—that is exactly how their ancestors treated the false prophets.
6.27: But to you who are listening I say: Love your enemies. Do good to those who hate you.
6.28: Speak well of those who curse you. Pray for those who treat you badly.
6.29: If someone strikes you on the cheek, offer the other one as well. If someone takes your outer coat, don't stop them from taking your shirt too.
6.30: Give to everyone who asks, and if someone takes what is yours, don't demand it back.
6.31: Treat others the way you want them to treat you.
6.32: If you only love those who love you back, what credit does that earn you? Even sinners love those who love them.
6.33: If you only do good to people who are good to you, what credit is that? Even sinners do the same.
6.34: And if you only lend to people you expect will repay you, what credit is that? Even sinners lend to sinners expecting to be paid back.
6.35: Instead, love your enemies, do good, and lend without expecting anything in return. Then your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High—because he is kind even to the ungrateful and the wicked.
6.36: Be compassionate, as your Father is compassionate.
6.37: Stop judging, and you will not be judged. Stop condemning, and you will not be condemned. Forgive, and you will be forgiven.
6.38: Give, and you will receive—a full measure, pressed down, shaken together, and overflowing will be poured into your lap. Because the same standard you apply to others will be applied back to you.
6.39: He told them this parable: Can a blind person lead another blind person? Won't they both fall into a ditch?
6.40: A student is not above their teacher, but when fully trained, a student will be like their teacher.
6.41: Why are you focused on the speck in your friend's eye when you have a plank in your own?
6.42: How can you say to your friend: Let me remove that speck from your eye—when you can't see the plank in your own? Hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you'll see clearly enough to remove the speck from your friend's.
6.43: A healthy tree doesn't bear bad fruit, and a diseased tree doesn't bear good fruit.
6.44: Each tree is known by its own fruit. You don't harvest figs from thornbushes or grapes from briars.
6.45: A good person draws good things from the good stored up in their heart, and an evil person draws evil from the evil stored up in theirs. For what the mouth says flows from what fills the heart.
6.46: Why do you keep calling me Lord, Lord—but don't do what I say?
6.47: Let me show you what the person who comes to me, hears my words, and puts them into practice is like:
6.48: He is like a man who built a house by digging deep and laying the foundation on solid rock. When the floods came and the torrent beat against that house, it could not be shaken—it was solidly built.
6.49: But the person who hears my words and doesn't act on them is like a man who built a house on bare ground without a foundation. When the flood came and the torrent struck, it collapsed instantly—and the destruction was total.

7.1: When Jesus had said everything in the hearing of the people, he entered Capernaum.
7.2: There a centurion's servant—a man his master held in great esteem—was gravely ill and near death.
7.3: When the centurion heard about Jesus, he sent Jewish elders to him, asking Jesus to come and heal his servant.
7.4: When they reached Jesus, they urged him with great urgency: This man deserves your help—
7.5: he loves our people and personally built our synagogue.
7.6: So Jesus went with them. He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends with this message: Lord, don't put yourself to this trouble—I am not worthy to have you enter my home.
7.7: That is why I did not come to you myself. Just speak the word, and my servant will be healed.
7.8: I myself am under authority and have soldiers who answer to me. I say to one: Go—and he goes. To another: Come—and he comes. To my servant: Do this—and it's done.
7.9: When Jesus heard this, he was astonished. Turning to the crowd following him, he said: I tell you, I have not found faith this great anywhere in Israel.
7.10: When the messengers returned to the house, they found the servant completely well.
7.11: Shortly after, Jesus traveled to a town called Nain, and his disciples and a large crowd went with him.
7.12: As he approached the town gate, a funeral procession was coming out—a young man, the only son of a widow. A large crowd from the town accompanied her.
7.13: When the Lord saw her, his heart broke for her. He said: Don't weep.
7.14: He stepped forward and touched the bier. The pallbearers stopped. He said: Young man—I tell you, get up.
7.15: The dead man sat up and began to speak. And Jesus gave him back to his mother.
7.16: Awe swept through the crowd. They praised God: A great prophet has risen among us! God has come to the aid of his people!
7.17: News of this spread through all Judea and the surrounding countryside.
7.18: John's disciples brought him word of all this. He summoned two of them
7.19: and sent them to the Lord with this question: Are you the one who was promised, or should we be looking for someone else?
7.20: When the men reached Jesus, they said: John the Baptist has sent us to ask you: Are you the Coming One, or should we wait for someone else?
7.21: At that very time Jesus was healing many people of diseases and afflictions and evil spirits, and giving sight to the blind.
7.22: He replied: Go and tell John what you have seen and heard—the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor hear good news.
7.23: And blessed is the one who is not tripped up by me.
7.24: When John's messengers left, Jesus began to speak to the crowds about John: What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed bending in the wind?
7.25: Then what did you go out to see? Someone dressed in fine clothes? No—people who wear fine clothing and live in luxury are found in palaces.
7.26: Then what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes—and I tell you, far more than a prophet.
7.27: This is the man Scripture speaks of: I am sending my messenger ahead of you to prepare your path.
7.28: I tell you, among all who have ever been born, none is greater than John—and yet the least person in God's kingdom is greater than he is.
7.29: (All the people who heard this—even the tax collectors—acknowledged that God was right, for they had been baptized by John.
7.30: But the Pharisees and religious lawyers, having refused John's baptism, rejected what God intended for them.)
7.31: Jesus continued: What can I compare this generation to? What does it look like?
7.32: They are like children sitting in the marketplace calling out to each other: We played a wedding song and you wouldn't dance; we sang a funeral song and you wouldn't cry.
7.33: John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say: He's demon-possessed.
7.34: The Son of Man comes eating and drinking, and you say: Look at him—a glutton and a drunk, a friend of tax collectors and sinners!
7.35: And yet wisdom is vindicated by all who are shaped by her.
7.36: One of the Pharisees invited Jesus to dinner, and he went to the man's home and reclined at the table.
7.37: A woman from that town who had lived a sinful life heard that Jesus was dining at the Pharisee's house. She came carrying an alabaster jar of perfume,
7.38: and standing behind Jesus at his feet, she wept. Her tears fell on his feet, and she wiped them with her hair. She kissed his feet and anointed them with the perfume.
7.39: The Pharisee who had invited him saw this and said to himself: If this man were really a prophet, he would know what kind of woman is touching him—that she is a sinner.
7.40: Jesus spoke up: Simon, I have something to say to you. He replied: Go ahead, Teacher.
7.41: Two people were in debt to the same moneylender. One owed five hundred denarii, the other fifty.
7.42: Neither could repay him, so he cancelled both debts. Now—which of them will love him more?
7.43: Simon answered: I suppose the one whose larger debt was cancelled. Jesus said: You are exactly right.
7.44: Then he turned to the woman and said to Simon: Do you see this woman? I came into your house—you gave me no water for my feet. But she has bathed my feet with her tears and dried them with her hair.
7.45: You gave me no greeting kiss—but she has not stopped kissing my feet since the moment I arrived.
7.46: You did not anoint my head with oil—but she has poured perfume on my feet.
7.47: Because of this I tell you: her many sins are forgiven—as her great love shows. But the one who has been forgiven little loves little.
7.48: Then he said to her: Your sins are forgiven.
7.49: The other guests began saying to one another: Who is this man, that he even forgives sins?
7.50: But Jesus said to the woman: Your faith has saved you. Go in peace.

8.1: After this Jesus traveled through towns and villages, announcing the good news of God's kingdom. The Twelve were with him,
8.2: and also some women who had been healed of evil spirits and sicknesses: Mary called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had been driven out;
8.3: Joanna, wife of Chuza who managed Herod's household; Susanna; and many others—women who supported Jesus and the Twelve out of their own resources.
8.4: As a large crowd was assembling—people streaming in from every town—Jesus told this parable:
8.5: A farmer went out to sow his seed. As he scattered it, some fell on the path, was trampled, and the birds ate it.
8.6: Some fell on rock, sprouted, then withered because it had no moisture.
8.7: Some fell among thorns—the thorns grew up alongside and choked it.
8.8: And some fell into good soil, grew up, and produced a hundredfold harvest. As he said this, he cried out: Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear!
8.9: His disciples asked him what the parable meant.
8.10: He said: You have been given insight into the mysteries of God's kingdom—but for others, everything comes in parables, so that seeing, they do not see; and hearing, they do not understand.
8.11: This is what the parable means: the seed is the word of God.
8.12: The seed on the path are people who hear the word—then the devil comes and snatches it from their hearts before they can believe and be saved.
8.13: The seed on rocky ground are those who receive the word with joy—but they have no root. They believe for a while, then fall away when testing comes.
8.14: The seed in the thorns are those who hear, but as they move through life the worries, wealth, and pleasures of this world crowd in and choke them—they never reach maturity.
8.15: The seed in good soil—these are people who hear the word, hold it in an honest and open heart, and by endurance bring forth a harvest.
8.16: No one lights a lamp and covers it with a bowl or pushes it under the bed. They put it on a stand so everyone who comes in can see the light.
8.17: For nothing is hidden that won't eventually be revealed, and nothing secret that won't be brought to light.
8.18: So pay careful attention to how you listen. Those who have will receive more; those who have nothing—even what they think they have will be taken.
8.19: Jesus's mother and brothers came to see him but could not get through the crowd.
8.20: Someone told him: Your mother and brothers are standing outside—they want to see you.
8.21: He replied: My mother and my brothers—these are the people who hear God's word and live it out.
8.22: One day Jesus said to his disciples: Let's cross to the other side of the lake. They got in a boat and pushed off.
8.23: As they sailed he fell asleep. A sudden squall swept down on the lake, the boat began filling with water, and they were in grave danger.
8.24: They woke him, crying: Master! Master! We're going to drown! He got up and rebuked the wind and the churning water—and they stilled. There was dead calm.
8.25: He said to them: Where is your faith? They were terrified and astonished, asking each other: Who is this man? He commands the wind and the waves—and they obey him!
8.26: They sailed on to the region of the Gerasenes, on the far shore from Galilee.
8.27: When Jesus stepped ashore, a man from the town met him—a man possessed by demons. For a long time he had worn no clothes and lived not in a house but among the tombs.
8.28: When he saw Jesus, he shrieked and fell at his feet, screaming: What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? Please don't torment me!
8.29: For Jesus had been commanding the unclean spirit to come out of the man. Many times it had seized him; though he was guarded and bound with chains and shackles, he would snap the bonds and be driven by the demon into the wilderness.
8.30: Jesus asked him: What is your name? He said: Legion—because many demons had entered him.
8.31: And they pleaded with Jesus not to command them into the abyss.
8.32: A large herd of pigs was feeding on the hillside. The demons begged to be allowed to enter them, and Jesus gave permission.
8.33: The demons came out of the man and went into the pigs. The whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the lake and drowned.
8.34: When the herdsmen saw what happened, they ran off and spread the news in town and countryside.
8.35: People came out to see what had happened. They found the man from whom the demons had left sitting at Jesus's feet—dressed and completely in his right mind. They were afraid.
8.36: Those who had witnessed it told them how the man had been healed.
8.37: Then the whole population of the surrounding Gerasene region asked Jesus to leave—they were gripped with great fear. So he got back in the boat and returned.
8.38: The man from whom the demons had gone begged to go with Jesus, but Jesus sent him away:
8.39: Go back home and tell everyone what God has done for you. So the man went and proclaimed throughout the whole city everything Jesus had done for him.
8.40: When Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed him—they had been waiting for him.
8.41: A man named Jairus—a leader of the synagogue—came and fell at Jesus's feet, begging him urgently to come to his home,
8.42: because his only daughter, about twelve years old, was dying. As Jesus went, the crowd pressed in around him.
8.43: In the crowd was a woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years. She had spent all she had on doctors but no one could cure her.
8.44: She came up behind Jesus and touched the hem of his cloak—and immediately her bleeding stopped.
8.45: Jesus said: Who touched me? Everyone denied it, and Peter said: Master, the crowds are surrounding you and pressing against you!
8.46: But Jesus said: Someone touched me—I felt power go out from me.
8.47: The woman realized she could not stay hidden. She came trembling, fell down before him, and declared in front of everyone why she had touched him—and how she had been instantly healed.
8.48: Jesus said to her: Daughter, your faith has healed you. Go in peace.
8.49: While he was still speaking, someone arrived from Jairus's house: Your daughter is dead. Don't trouble the Teacher any further.
8.50: Jesus heard this and said to Jairus: Don't be afraid—only believe, and she will be well.
8.51: When he reached the house, he allowed no one inside except Peter, John, and James, along with the child's father and mother.
8.52: Everyone was weeping and lamenting for her. He said: Stop weeping—she is not dead, only sleeping.
8.53: They laughed at him, for they knew she had died.
8.54: He took her by the hand and called out: Child—get up.
8.55: Her spirit returned and she stood up at once. He instructed them to give her something to eat.
8.56: Her parents were overwhelmed with astonishment, but he directed them to tell no one what had happened.

9.1: Calling the Twelve together, Jesus gave them power and authority over all demons and the ability to heal diseases.
9.2: He sent them out to announce God's kingdom and to heal.
9.3: He instructed them: Take nothing for the journey—no walking stick, no bag, no bread, no money, not even a spare shirt.
9.4: Whatever home you enter, stay there until you leave that place.
9.5: If a town will not receive you, shake the dust from your feet as you leave—a witness against them.
9.6: So they went out, traveling from village to village—proclaiming the good news and healing everywhere they went.
9.7: Herod the ruler heard about everything that was happening and was thoroughly perplexed—some people were saying John had been raised from the dead,
9.8: others that Elijah had appeared, others that one of the ancient prophets had come back to life.
9.9: Herod said: John I beheaded myself. So who is this that I keep hearing about? And he sought to see Jesus.
9.10: When the apostles returned, they reported to Jesus all they had done. He took them along and withdrew privately to a town called Bethsaida.
9.11: But the crowds found out and followed. He welcomed them, spoke to them about God's kingdom, and healed those who needed healing.
9.12: As the day was drawing to a close, the Twelve came to him: Send the crowd away so they can find food and lodging in the surrounding villages and countryside—there is nothing out here.
9.13: He said: You give them something to eat. They answered: We have nothing but five loaves and two fish—unless we go and buy food for all these people.
9.14: There were about five thousand men. He said to his disciples: Have them sit down in groups of about fifty.
9.15: They did this, and everyone sat down.
9.16: He took the five loaves and two fish, looked up toward heaven, and gave thanks. He broke the loaves and gave them to the disciples to set before the crowd.
9.17: Everyone ate and was satisfied. The leftover pieces filled twelve baskets.
9.18: One day as Jesus was praying alone, his disciples were nearby. He asked them: Who do the crowds say I am?
9.19: They replied: Some say John the Baptist. Others say Elijah, and others that one of the ancient prophets has come back to life.
9.20: He said: But you—who do you say I am? Peter answered: The Messiah of God.
9.21: Jesus gave them strict orders to tell this to no one.
9.22: He told them: The Son of Man must endure great suffering—be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and teachers of the law—and be killed, and on the third day be raised to life.
9.23: Then he said to everyone: If anyone wants to follow me, they must say no to themselves, take up their cross every day, and follow me.
9.24: Whoever tries to preserve their life will lose it—but whoever gives up their life for my sake will save it.
9.25: What does it gain anyone to win the whole world and destroy themselves in the process?
9.26: Whoever is ashamed of me and my words—the Son of Man will be ashamed of them when he comes in his glory and the glory of the Father and the holy angels.
9.27: I tell you truly: some standing here will not die before they see the kingdom of God.
9.28: About eight days after saying these things, Jesus took Peter, John, and James and went up the mountain to pray.
9.29: As he prayed, the appearance of his face changed and his clothing became dazzling white.
9.30: Two men appeared in radiant glory, talking with him—Moses and Elijah.
9.31: They were speaking about his departure—the exodus he was about to complete in Jerusalem.
9.32: Peter and the others were heavy with sleep, but when they fully awoke they saw his glory—and the two men standing with him.
9.33: As Moses and Elijah were withdrawing, Peter said to Jesus: Master, it is so good that we are here! Let us put up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah. He did not know what he was saying.
9.34: While he was still speaking, a cloud came and enveloped them—and they were afraid as they entered the cloud.
9.35: A voice came from the cloud: This is my Son, my Chosen One—listen to him!
9.36: When the voice had spoken, Jesus was there alone. They kept this to themselves and told no one in those days what they had seen.
9.37: The next day, as they came down from the mountain, a large crowd met him.
9.38: A man in the crowd cried out: Teacher—I beg you, look at my son. He is my only child.
9.39: A spirit grabs him and suddenly he screams. It throws him into convulsions, he foams at the mouth—it batters him and barely lets him go.
9.40: I begged your disciples to cast it out—but they couldn't.
9.41: Jesus replied: What an unbelieving, stubborn generation. How long must I stay with you and put up with you? Bring your son here.
9.42: Even while the boy was approaching, the demon threw him down in a convulsion. Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the boy, and gave him back to his father.
9.43: Everyone was overwhelmed with awe at the greatness of God. And while they were all marveling at everything he did, Jesus said to his disciples:
9.44: Let these words go deep into your ears: The Son of Man is about to be handed over into human hands.
9.45: But they did not understand what he meant—it was hidden from them so they could not grasp it—and they were afraid to ask him about it.
9.46: An argument broke out among them over which of them was the greatest.
9.47: Jesus knew what they were thinking. He took a small child and had the child stand beside him.
9.48: He said: Whoever welcomes this child in my name welcomes me—and whoever welcomes me welcomes the one who sent me. Because the one who is least among you all is the truly great one.
9.49: John said: Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name and we tried to stop him, because he doesn't travel with us.
9.50: Jesus replied: Don't stop him—whoever is not against you is for you.
9.51: As the time drew near for him to be taken up, Jesus set his face with determination toward Jerusalem.
9.52: He sent messengers ahead of him who went into a Samaritan village to prepare for his arrival.
9.53: But the villagers would not welcome him, because his destination was Jerusalem.
9.54: When James and John saw this, they said: Lord, do you want us to call down fire from heaven to destroy them?
9.56: And they went on to another village.
9.57: As they were walking along the road, someone said to him: I will follow you wherever you go.
9.58: Jesus replied: Foxes have their dens and birds have their nests—but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head.
9.59: To another he said: Follow me. But the man replied: Lord, let me first go and bury my father.
9.60: Jesus said: Let the dead bury their own dead. You—go and announce the kingdom of God.
9.61: Another said: I will follow you, Lord—but first let me say goodbye to my family.
9.62: Jesus said: No one who takes hold of the plow and keeps looking back is fit for the kingdom of God.

10.1: After this the Lord appointed seventy-two others and sent them ahead of him in pairs to every town and place he was about to visit.
10.2: He told them: The harvest is enormous but the workers are few. So ask the Lord of the harvest to send workers out into his fields.
10.3: Go—I am sending you out like lambs among wolves.
10.4: Take no money bag, no pack, no sandals—and don't stop to greet anyone along the road.
10.5: When you enter a home, first say: Peace to this house.
10.6: If someone there is receptive to peace, your peace will settle on them. If not, it will return to you.
10.7: Stay in that same house, eating and drinking whatever they provide—the worker deserves their pay. Don't keep moving from house to house.
10.8: Whenever you enter a town and are welcomed, eat whatever is placed before you.
10.9: Heal the sick there and tell them: The kingdom of God has drawn near to you.
10.10: But when you enter a town and are not welcomed, go into its streets and say:
10.11: Even the dust of your town we wipe from our feet—as a witness against you. But know this: the kingdom of God has come near.
10.12: I tell you, on that day Sodom will fare better than that town.
10.13: Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! If the mighty works done among you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have turned to God long ago in sackcloth and ashes.
10.14: But at the judgment, Tyre and Sidon will be better off than you.
10.15: And you, Capernaum—do you think you will be lifted to heaven? You will be brought down to the depths.
10.16: Whoever listens to you listens to me. Whoever rejects you rejects me—and whoever rejects me rejects the one who sent me.
10.17: The seventy-two came back full of joy: Lord, even the demons submit to us when we use your name!
10.18: He said to them: I was watching and saw Satan fall like lightning from the sky.
10.19: I have given you authority to trample on snakes and scorpions and over the full power of the enemy—nothing will harm you.
10.20: But don't celebrate because the spirits submit to you—celebrate because your names are written in heaven.
10.21: At that very moment Jesus overflowed with joy in the Holy Spirit and said: I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned and revealed them to children. Yes, Father—this was your good pleasure.
10.22: My Father has entrusted everything to me. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son—and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.
10.23: Turning privately to his disciples, he said: How blessed are the eyes that see what you are seeing!
10.24: I tell you, many prophets and kings longed to see what you are seeing and did not see it—to hear what you are hearing and did not hear it.
10.25: A legal expert stood up to test Jesus: Teacher, what must I do to inherit the life of the coming age?
10.26: Jesus replied: What does the Law say? How do you read it?
10.27: He answered: Love the Lord your God with all your heart, all your soul, all your strength, and all your mind—and love your neighbor as yourself.
10.28: You've answered well, Jesus said. Do this and you will truly live.
10.29: But wanting to justify himself, he asked: And who exactly is my neighbor?
10.30: Jesus replied: A man was traveling down from Jerusalem to Jericho when robbers attacked him. They stripped him, beat him, and left him half dead on the road.
10.31: By chance a priest came down that same road. He saw the man—and passed by on the other side.
10.32: A Levite came the same way, looked at the man—and also passed by on the other side.
10.33: But a Samaritan, traveling that same road, came upon the man—and when he saw him, he was moved with deep compassion.
10.34: He went to him, poured oil and wine on his wounds and bandaged them. He lifted the man onto his own animal, brought him to an inn, and cared for him through the night.
10.35: The next morning he took out two silver coins and gave them to the innkeeper: Look after him, he said, and when I come back I'll pay you for any additional expense.
10.36: Now—which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who was attacked?
10.37: The man answered: The one who showed him mercy. Jesus said: Go and do the same.
10.38: As they continued on their way, Jesus entered a village where a woman named Martha welcomed him into her home.
10.39: She had a sister named Mary, who sat at the Lord's feet and listened to his teaching.
10.40: Martha, though, was pulled in every direction by all the preparations. She came to Jesus and said: Lord, doesn't it concern you that my sister has left me to do all the work alone? Tell her to help me!
10.41: The Lord answered: Martha, Martha—you are anxious and troubled about so many things.
10.42: But only one thing is truly necessary. Mary has chosen the better part—and it will not be taken from her.

11.1: Jesus was praying in a certain place. When he finished, one of his disciples said: Lord, teach us to pray, the way John taught his disciples.
11.2: He said: When you pray, say this: Father—holy is your name. May your kingdom come.
11.3: Give us each day the bread we need.
11.4: Forgive us our sins—for we ourselves forgive everyone who wrongs us. And do not lead us into the time of trial.
11.5: He said: Suppose one of you has a friend. You go to him at midnight and say: Friend, lend me three loaves of bread—
11.6: a friend of mine just arrived after a long journey and I have nothing to feed him.
11.7: And suppose the one inside says: Don't bother me—the door is already bolted, and my children are with me in bed. I can't get up to give you anything.
11.8: I tell you: even if he won't get up out of friendship, his neighbor's bold persistence will get him up to give him as much as he needs.
11.9: So I say to you: Ask, and you will receive. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and the door will open to you.
11.10: For everyone who asks receives; everyone who seeks finds; and to everyone who knocks, the door opens.
11.11: What father among you—if his son asks for a fish, will hand him a snake?
11.12: Or if he asks for an egg, will give him a scorpion?
11.13: If you, flawed as you are, know how to give good gifts to your children—how much more will your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!
11.14: Jesus was driving out a demon that had made a man mute. When the demon came out, the man spoke—and the crowd was amazed.
11.15: But some of them said: He casts out demons by Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons.
11.16: Others, trying to trap him, demanded a sign from heaven.
11.17: Knowing their thoughts, he said: Any kingdom torn apart by civil war collapses—and a house split against itself falls.
11.18: If Satan is divided against himself, how does his kingdom survive? And yet you say I cast out demons by Beelzebul.
11.19: If I cast out demons by Beelzebul, then by whom do your own people cast them out? They will be your judges on that account.
11.20: But if I am casting out demons by the finger of God, then the kingdom of God has arrived among you.
11.21: When a fully armed strong man guards his own estate, his property is secure.
11.22: But when someone stronger attacks and defeats him, that conqueror strips away the armor he relied on and divides the plunder.
11.23: Whoever is not with me is against me—and whoever does not gather with me scatters.
11.24: When an unclean spirit leaves a person, it wanders through waterless wastelands looking for rest. Finding none, it says: I'll go back to the house I left.
11.25: When it arrives, it finds the house swept clean and tidy.
11.26: So it goes and brings back seven spirits worse than itself, and they all move in and take up residence. The final state of that person is far worse than the beginning.
11.27: While Jesus was saying these things, a woman in the crowd called out: Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts that fed you!
11.28: He replied: Blessed, rather, are those who hear God's word and live by it.
11.29: As the crowds swelled, he said: This generation is evil. It demands a sign—but no sign will be given it except the sign of Jonah.
11.30: For just as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so the Son of Man will be a sign to this generation.
11.31: The Queen of the South will stand up at the judgment alongside this generation and condemn it—she came from the far ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom, and something far greater than Solomon is standing here now.
11.32: The people of Nineveh will stand at the judgment alongside this generation and condemn it—they turned to God at the preaching of Jonah, and something far greater than Jonah is here.
11.33: No one lights a lamp and hides it in the cellar or covers it with a bowl—they put it on a stand so that everyone who enters can see the light.
11.34: Your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eye is clear and open, your whole body fills with light. When it is clouded, your whole body is in darkness.
11.35: So take care that the light inside you is not actually darkness.
11.36: If your whole body is filled with light—not a dark corner anywhere—it will be as bright as when a lamp illuminates you with its full radiance.
11.37: When Jesus finished speaking, a Pharisee invited him to dine with him. He went in and reclined at the table.
11.38: The Pharisee was astonished to see that Jesus did not wash before eating.
11.39: The Lord said to him: You Pharisees—you clean the outside of the cup and the plate, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness.
11.40: What fools! Didn't the same God who made the outside also make the inside?
11.41: Give what is inside as generosity to the poor—and everything becomes clean for you.
11.42: But woe to you Pharisees! You meticulously tithe your mint and rue and every herb—but you pass right over justice and the love of God. You should have practiced these without abandoning the others.
11.43: Woe to you Pharisees! You love the seat of honor in the synagogues and being greeted with respect in the public squares.
11.44: Woe to you—you are like unmarked graves that people walk over without knowing they're there.
11.45: One of the religious lawyers said: Teacher, when you say these things, you insult us as well.
11.46: Jesus replied: Woe to you lawyers too! You pile crushing burdens on people's backs—and you won't lift a finger to help carry them.
11.47: Woe to you! You build monuments for the prophets—the very prophets your ancestors murdered.
11.48: By doing this you are witnesses who approve of what your ancestors did—they did the killing, you do the monument-building.
11.49: This is why God's wisdom declared: I will send them prophets and messengers—some they will kill, others they will persecute.
11.50: As a result, this generation will be held accountable for the blood of every prophet shed since the world began—
11.51: from Abel's blood to Zechariah's, who was killed between the altar and the sanctuary. Yes—I tell you, this generation will be held accountable for all of it.
11.52: Woe to you lawyers! You have taken away the key to understanding—you didn't go in yourselves and you blocked the way for those trying to enter.
11.53: When Jesus left that house, the religious scholars and Pharisees began pressing him hard and barraging him with questions,
11.54: setting traps to catch him in his own words.

12.1: Meanwhile, as tens of thousands pressed in around him so tightly they were trampling each other, Jesus spoke first to his disciples: Guard yourselves against the leaven of the Pharisees—their hypocrisy.
12.2: Nothing covered will stay covered; nothing hidden will stay hidden.
12.3: What you have spoken in the dark will be heard in the light—what you have whispered in secret rooms will be proclaimed from the rooftops.
12.4: I tell you, my friends: do not fear those who can kill the body but can do nothing more after that.
12.5: But I will tell you whom to fear—fear the one who, after death, has authority to cast you into Gehenna. Yes, fear him.
12.6: Are five sparrows not sold for two pennies? And yet not one of them is forgotten by God.
12.7: The very hairs of your head are all counted. Don't be afraid—you matter far more to God than many sparrows.
12.8: I tell you: everyone who acknowledges me before others, the Son of Man will acknowledge before God's angels.
12.9: But whoever disowns me before others will be disowned before God's angels.
12.10: Anyone who speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven—but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.
12.11: When they haul you before synagogues and rulers and officials, do not worry about how to defend yourself or what to say.
12.12: The Holy Spirit will teach you in that very moment exactly what to say.
12.13: Someone in the crowd called out: Teacher, tell my brother to share the inheritance with me.
12.14: Jesus replied: Friend, who made me a judge or mediator between you?
12.15: Then he said to everyone: Watch out—be on your guard against every form of greed. A person's life does not consist in the abundance of their possessions.
12.16: He told them a parable: A certain rich man's land produced an extraordinary harvest.
12.17: He thought to himself: What am I to do? I have nowhere to store all this grain.
12.18: Then he said: I know what I'll do—I'll tear down my barns and build bigger ones, and I'll store all my grain and goods there.
12.19: Then I'll say to myself: You have everything you need stored up for years to come. Relax—eat, drink, enjoy life.
12.20: But God said to him: You fool! This very night your life will be required of you. Then who gets everything you have accumulated?
12.21: That is the end of the one who piles up treasure for themselves but is not rich in the things that matter to God.
12.22: He said to his disciples: This is why I tell you—don't be anxious about your life, what you'll eat, or about your body, what you'll wear.
12.23: Life is more than food, and the body more than clothes.
12.24: Look at the ravens—they don't plant or harvest, they have no storeroom or barn, and yet God feeds them. How much more valuable are you than birds!
12.25: And can any of you add a single hour to your life by worrying about it?
12.26: If you can't manage even this smallest thing, why worry about everything else?
12.27: Consider how the wildflowers grow—they don't work or weave. Yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his magnificence was not clothed like one of them.
12.28: If God clothes the grass that is here today and in the fire tomorrow—how much more will he clothe you! You of such small faith.
12.29: So don't set your heart on what you will eat or drink—don't be anxious about it.
12.30: The nations of the world chase after all these things—but your Father knows you need them.
12.31: Instead, seek his kingdom—and all these things will be given to you as well.
12.32: Don't be afraid, little flock—it is your Father's great delight to give you the kingdom.
12.33: Sell your possessions and give to the poor. Build yourselves purses that never wear out—a treasure in heaven that never fails, where no thief can reach it and no moth destroy it.
12.34: For wherever your treasure is, that is where your heart will be.
12.35: Stay ready for action—belts fastened, lamps burning.
12.36: Be like servants waiting for their master to return from a wedding banquet—so that when he comes and knocks, you open the door immediately.
12.37: Blessed are those servants the master finds alert when he arrives. I tell you truly—he will fasten his robe, seat them at the table, and come and serve them himself.
12.38: Whether he comes in the middle of the night or close to dawn—if he finds them awake and ready, those servants are blessed.
12.39: But know this—if the homeowner had known the hour the thief would come, he would not have allowed his house to be broken into.
12.40: You must be ready too—because the Son of Man is coming at an hour when you least expect it.
12.41: Peter asked: Lord, is this parable meant for us, or for everyone?
12.42: The Lord replied: Who then is the faithful, wise steward—the one the master will put in charge of his household to give each person their portion at the right time?
12.43: Blessed is that servant found doing this faithfully when the master arrives.
12.44: I tell you truly—he will put that servant over everything he owns.
12.45: But suppose that servant says to himself: My master won't be back for a while—and begins to beat the other servants and eat and drink and get drunk.
12.46: That servant's master will come on a day and at an hour he is not expecting—and will cut him to pieces and assign him a place among the faithless.
12.47: The servant who knew the master's will but made no preparation and did not act on it will receive a severe punishment.
12.48: The one who acted badly without knowing better will be punished less severely. From everyone who has been given much, much will be required—and from those entrusted with more, even more will be expected.
12.49: I came to throw fire on the earth—and how I wish it were already blazing!
12.50: I have a baptism to undergo—and what anguish grips me until it is completed!
12.51: Do you think I came to bring peace to the earth? No—I came to bring division.
12.52: From now on, five in one household will be divided—three against two and two against three.
12.53: Father against son, son against father; mother against daughter, daughter against mother; mother-in-law against daughter-in-law, daughter-in-law against mother-in-law.
12.54: He said to the crowds: When you see a cloud building in the west, you immediately say: Rain is coming—and it does.
12.55: And when the south wind blows, you say: It will be scorching—and it is.
12.56: Hypocrites! You can read the face of earth and sky—why can't you read what this moment means?
12.57: Why don't you judge for yourselves what the right thing is?
12.58: When you are on your way to court with your opponent, make every effort to settle matters before you get there—or he may drag you before the judge, the judge hand you to the officer, and the officer throw you in prison.
12.59: I tell you, you will not get out until you have paid to the very last coin.

13.1: Some people there told him about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
13.2: Jesus replied: Do you suppose these Galileans were greater sinners than all other Galileans, because they suffered this way?
13.3: No—I tell you. But unless you turn to God, you will all perish just as they did.
13.4: Or those eighteen people on whom the tower of Siloam fell and killed them—do you think they were greater offenders than everyone else in Jerusalem?
13.5: No—I tell you. But unless you turn to God, you will all perish just like them.
13.6: He told this parable: A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard. He came looking for fruit on it—and found none.
13.7: He said to the gardener: Look—for three years I've come looking for fruit on this tree and found nothing. Cut it down. Why should it go on wasting the soil?
13.8: The gardener answered: Sir, give it one more year. I'll dig around it and add fertilizer.
13.9: If it bears fruit next year—good. If not, then cut it down.
13.10: One Sabbath Jesus was teaching in a synagogue,
13.11: and a woman was there who had been bound by a crippling spirit for eighteen years. She was bent double and utterly unable to straighten herself.
13.12: When Jesus saw her, he called her to him and said: Woman, you are released from your condition.
13.13: He laid his hands on her—and immediately she straightened up and praised God.
13.14: The synagogue ruler, furious that Jesus had healed on the Sabbath, said to the crowd: There are six days for work—come to be healed on those days, not on the Sabbath.
13.15: The Lord answered: Hypocrites! Every one of you unties your ox or donkey from the stall on the Sabbath and leads it to water.
13.16: And ought not this woman—a daughter of Abraham whom Satan has kept bound for eighteen years—be set free from her bondage on the Sabbath?
13.17: When he said this, all his opponents were humiliated—but the whole crowd rejoiced at all the wonderful things he was doing.
13.18: Then he said: What is the kingdom of God like? To what shall I compare it?
13.19: It is like a mustard seed that a man planted in his garden. It grew and became a tree, and the birds came and nested in its branches.
13.20: Again he said: What shall I compare the kingdom of God to?
13.21: It is like yeast that a woman worked into a large batch of flour—until it had spread through the whole mass.
13.22: Jesus traveled through towns and villages, teaching as he journeyed toward Jerusalem.
13.23: Someone asked him: Lord, will only a few be saved? He replied:
13.24: Strive to enter through the narrow door—because many will try and will not be able.
13.25: Once the master of the house gets up and bolts the door, and you stand outside knocking—Lord, open up for us!—he will answer: I don't know where you come from.
13.26: Then you will say: We ate and drank with you—you taught in our streets!
13.27: But he will say: I don't know you or where you come from. Get away from me—all you workers of evil.
13.28: There will be weeping and grinding of teeth when you see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets inside the kingdom of God—and yourselves thrown out.
13.29: People will come from east and west and north and south and take their places at the feast in God's kingdom.
13.30: And there are those who are last who will be first—and first who will be last.
13.31: At that moment some Pharisees came to him: Leave here and go somewhere else—Herod wants to kill you.
13.32: He said: Go and tell that fox—I am driving out demons and performing healings today and tomorrow, and on the third day I complete my work.
13.33: But I must press on—today, tomorrow, and the day after—for it is unthinkable that a prophet should perish anywhere but Jerusalem.
13.34: Jerusalem, Jerusalem—you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you! How many times I have longed to gather your children together the way a hen gathers her chicks under her wings—and you would not let me.
13.35: Look—your house is abandoned to you. I tell you, you will not see me until the time comes when you say: Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.

14.1: One Sabbath, Jesus went to eat at the home of a leading Pharisee—and they were watching him closely.
14.2: Right there before him was a man with dropsy.
14.3: Jesus said to the lawyers and Pharisees: Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not?
14.4: They were silent. So Jesus took hold of the man, healed him, and sent him on his way.
14.5: Then he asked them: If your child or your ox falls into a well on the Sabbath—which of you would not pull them out immediately?
14.7: He noticed how the guests were choosing the places of honor, and told them this parable:
14.8: When someone invites you to a wedding banquet, don't take the place of honor—someone more distinguished than you may have been invited,
14.9: and the host who invited you both may come and say to you: Give this person your seat. Then in humiliation you will have to move to the lowest place.
14.10: But when you are invited, take the lowest place—so that when your host comes he can say: Friend, move up to a better seat. Then you will be honored in front of everyone at the table.
14.11: For everyone who exalts himself will be brought low—and everyone who humbles himself will be lifted up.
14.12: He also said to the man who had invited him: When you host a lunch or dinner, don't invite your friends, brothers, relatives, or wealthy neighbors—they will invite you back, and you will be repaid.
14.13: Instead, when you give a feast, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind.
14.14: Then you will be truly blessed—because they cannot repay you. You will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.
14.15: One of the guests who heard this said: Blessed is everyone who will eat at the banquet in God's kingdom!
14.16: Jesus replied: A man once prepared a great banquet and invited many guests.
14.17: When the time came, he sent his servant to tell the invited guests: Come—everything is ready now.
14.18: But they all—every one—began making excuses. The first said: I have just bought a field and need to go inspect it. Please excuse me.
14.19: Another said: I've just bought five yoke of oxen and I'm going to try them out. Please excuse me.
14.20: Another said: I have just married—I can't come.
14.21: The servant returned and reported this to the master. Angry, the master told his servant: Go out immediately into the streets and alleys of the city—bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.
14.22: The servant said: Sir, what you ordered has been done—and there is still room.
14.23: The master said: Go out to the roads and the hedgerows and bring people in—persuade them to come—so that my house will be full.
14.24: I tell you, not one of those originally invited will taste a single bite of my banquet.
14.25: Large crowds were traveling with him. He turned and said to them:
14.26: If anyone comes to me without placing me before father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters—yes, even their own life—that person cannot be my disciple.
14.27: And whoever does not carry their own cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
14.28: Which of you, wanting to build a tower, doesn't first sit down and calculate the cost to see if you have enough to finish it?
14.29: If you lay the foundation but can't finish, everyone who sees it will mock you—
14.30: saying: This person started building and couldn't complete it.
14.31: Or what king, heading into war against another king, doesn't first sit down and think through whether his ten thousand troops can face the other's twenty thousand?
14.32: If he can't, he sends envoys while the enemy is still far off and asks for terms of peace.
14.33: In the same way, any of you who does not give up everything they have cannot be my disciple.
14.34: Salt is good—but if salt loses its flavor, what can restore it?
14.35: It is useless for the soil or the compost heap—it gets thrown away. Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear.

15.1: All the tax collectors and sinners were crowding close to hear Jesus.
15.2: The Pharisees and religious scholars grumbled: This man welcomes sinners and actually eats with them.
15.3: So he told them this parable:
15.4: Suppose you have a hundred sheep and lose one. Don't you leave the ninety-nine out in the open and go after the lost one until you find it?
15.5: And when you find it, you lift it onto your shoulders with joy.
15.6: You come home and call together your friends and neighbors: Celebrate with me—I have found my lost sheep!
15.7: I tell you—there is more joy in heaven over one sinner who turns back to God than over ninety-nine righteous people who have no need to repent.
15.8: Or suppose a woman has ten silver coins and loses one. Doesn't she light a lamp and sweep the whole house, searching carefully until she finds it?
