15.9: And when she finds it, she calls her friends and neighbors: Celebrate with me—I found the coin I had lost!
15.10: I tell you, there is joy among God's angels over one sinner who turns back to God.
15.11: Jesus continued: A man had two sons.
15.12: The younger said to his father: Father, give me my share of the estate. So the father divided his property between them.
15.13: Not many days later, the younger son packed everything up and left for a distant country—where he wasted his entire inheritance in reckless living.
15.14: After he had spent everything, a severe famine struck that country—and he began to go hungry.
15.15: He went and attached himself to a citizen of that country, who sent him out to feed pigs.
15.16: He was so hungry he would gladly have eaten the pigs' food—but no one gave him anything.
15.17: Then he came to his senses. He said: How many of my father's hired workers have more than enough to eat—and here I am dying of hunger!
15.18: I will get up and go to my father and say: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you.
15.19: I am no longer worthy to be called your son—make me like one of your hired workers.
15.20: So he got up and set out toward his father. While he was still far down the road, his father saw him—his heart broke with compassion. He ran, threw his arms around him, and kissed him.
15.21: The son said: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.
15.22: But the father said to his servants: Quick—bring the best robe and put it on him! Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet.
15.23: Bring the fattened calf and slaughter it—let's eat and celebrate!
15.24: For this son of mine was dead—and is alive again! He was lost—and is found! And the celebration began.
15.25: His older son was out in the field. As he came near the house, he heard music and dancing.
15.26: He called one of the servants and asked what was happening.
15.27: The servant said: Your brother has come back—your father has slaughtered the fattened calf because he has him home safe and sound.
15.28: The older brother was furious and refused to go in. His father came out and pleaded with him.
15.29: But he answered his father: Look—all these years I have worked for you like a slave and never once disobeyed your orders. You never gave me even a goat to celebrate with my friends.
15.30: But when this son of yours shows up—the one who has blown your money on prostitutes—you slaughter the fattened calf for him!
15.31: His father said: Son—you are always with me. Everything I have is yours.
15.32: But we had to celebrate—we had to be glad. Your brother was dead and is alive. He was lost and is found.

16.1: He said to his disciples: There was a rich man whose manager was charged with squandering his property.
16.2: He called him in and said: What is this I hear about you? Turn in your accounts—you can no longer be my manager.
16.3: The manager said to himself: What am I going to do? My master is removing me from this position. I'm not strong enough to dig—and I'm ashamed to beg.
16.4: I know exactly what I'll do—so that when I am put out of this position, people will welcome me into their homes.
16.5: He called in each of his master's debtors one by one. To the first he said: How much do you owe my master?
16.6: A hundred jars of oil, the man said. The manager told him: Take your bill—sit down quickly and make it fifty.
16.7: He said to another: And how much do you owe? A hundred sacks of wheat. Write eighty, he told him.
16.8: The master praised the dishonest manager for acting with such street-smart shrewdness. The people of this world are wiser in dealing with their own kind than the people of light.
16.9: I tell you—use your worldly wealth to make friends, so that when it runs out, you will be welcomed into homes that last forever.
16.10: Whoever is trustworthy in the smallest things is trustworthy in larger ones—and whoever is dishonest in small matters will be dishonest in larger ones too.
16.11: If you haven't been faithful with dishonest wealth, who will entrust you with what is genuinely valuable?
16.12: And if you haven't been faithful with what belongs to someone else, who will give you anything that is truly yours?
16.13: No servant can serve two masters. Either you will hate one and love the other, or you will be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and money.
16.14: The Pharisees—lovers of money—heard all this and scoffed at him.
16.15: He said to them: You are the ones who make yourselves look righteous before people—but God sees your hearts. What human beings prize is an abomination in God's sight.
16.16: The Law and the Prophets pointed forward until John. From then on, the good news of God's kingdom is being announced—and everyone presses urgently into it.
16.17: It is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for the smallest stroke of the Law to become void.
16.18: Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery—and whoever marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.
16.19: There was a rich man who dressed in purple and fine linen and feasted lavishly every single day.
16.20: At his gate lay a poor man named Lazarus—covered with sores,
16.21: longing to be fed with whatever scraps fell from the rich man's table. Even the dogs came and licked his sores.
16.22: The beggar died, and the angels carried him to Abraham's side. The rich man also died—and was buried.
16.23: In Hades, in the depths of his torment, he looked up and saw Abraham—far away—with Lazarus at his side.
16.24: He called out: Father Abraham, have mercy on me! Send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue—I am in agony in this fire!
16.25: But Abraham said: Child, remember—in your lifetime you received every good thing, and Lazarus received every hard thing. Now he is comforted here, and you are in agony.
16.26: And besides all this, a great chasm has been fixed between us and you—so that no one who wishes to cross from here to you is able, and no one can cross from there to reach us.
16.27: He said: Then I beg you, father—send him to my father's household.
16.28: I have five brothers—let him warn them, so they will not also end up in this place of torment.
16.29: Abraham said: They have Moses and the Prophets—let them listen to them.
16.30: No, father Abraham, he said—but if someone from the dead goes to them, they will turn back to God.
16.31: He replied: If they will not hear Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead.

17.1: He said to his disciples: It is inevitable that stumbling blocks will come—but woe to the one through whom they come.
17.2: Better to be thrown into the sea with a millstone around your neck than to cause one of these little ones to fall.
17.3: Watch yourselves carefully. If a brother or sister sins, rebuke them—and if they repent, forgive them.
17.4: Even if they sin against you seven times in a single day—and seven times turn back saying: I repent—you must forgive them.
17.5: The apostles said to the Lord: Give us more faith.
17.6: The Lord said: If you had faith even as small as a mustard seed, you could say to this mulberry tree: Be pulled up and planted in the sea—and it would obey you.
17.7: Suppose one of you has a servant ploughing or tending sheep. When the servant comes in from the field, would you say: Come in right now and sit down to eat?
17.8: No—you would say: Prepare my supper, get yourself ready, and serve me while I eat and drink. After that you may eat and drink.
17.9: Does he thank the servant for doing what he was told to do? Of course not.
17.10: In the same way, when you have done everything you were ordered to do, say: We are unworthy servants—we have only done what was required of us.
17.11: As he made his way to Jerusalem, Jesus traveled along the border region between Samaria and Galilee.
17.12: Entering a village, he was met by ten men with leprosy. They stood at a distance
17.13: and called out with one voice: Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!
17.14: When he saw them, he said: Go—show yourselves to the priests. And as they went, they were cleansed.
17.15: One of them, seeing that he had been healed, turned back—praising God with a loud voice.
17.16: He fell at Jesus' feet, face down, and gave him thanks. He was a Samaritan.
17.17: Jesus said: Were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine?
17.18: Was no one found to turn back and give glory to God—except this outsider?
17.19: He said to him: Rise and go—your faith has healed you.
17.20: The Pharisees asked him when the kingdom of God would come. He answered: The kingdom of God does not come in ways that can be tracked and watched;
17.21: no one will say: Look, here it is! or There! For the kingdom of God is already in your midst.
17.22: He said to his disciples: Days are coming when you will long to see just one of the days of the Son of Man—and you will not see it.
17.23: They will say: Look, there! or Look, here! Don't go running after them.
17.24: For just as lightning flashes and lights up the sky from one horizon to the other, so will the Son of Man be in his day.
17.25: But first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation.
17.26: Just as things were in the days of Noah, so it will be in the days of the Son of Man.
17.27: They ate, they drank, they married and were given in marriage—right up to the day Noah entered the ark. Then the flood came and swept them all away.
17.28: It was the same in the days of Lot. They ate and drank, bought and sold, planted and built—
17.29: but on the day Lot walked out of Sodom, fire and sulfur rained from heaven and destroyed them all.
17.30: So it will be on the day the Son of Man is revealed.
17.31: On that day, if you are on the roof, don't go down to take anything from inside. And if you are in the field, don't turn back.
17.33: Whoever tries to hold on to their life will lose it—but whoever lets their life go will keep it.
17.34: I tell you—in that night, two will be lying in one bed. One will be taken, the other left.
17.35: Two women will be grinding grain together—one will be taken, the other left.
17.36: Two men will be in the field—one will be taken, the other left.
17.37: Where will this happen, Lord? they asked. He said: Where the corpse is, there the eagles will flock together.

18.1: He told them a parable about why they should always pray and never give up.
18.2: He said: In a certain city there was a judge who had no fear of God and no concern for people.
18.3: In that city was a widow who kept coming to him, pleading: Give me justice against my opponent.
18.4: For a while he refused. But at last he said to himself: I care nothing for God or for people—
18.5: but because this widow won't stop troubling me, I will give her justice—before she finally wears me out completely.
18.6: The Lord said: Listen to what this unjust judge says.
18.7: Will not God avenge his chosen ones who cry out to him day and night? Will he keep putting them off?
18.8: I tell you, he will give them justice—and quickly. But when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on earth?
18.9: He told this parable to some who were sure of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else:
18.10: Two men went up to the temple to pray—one a Pharisee, the other a tax collector.
18.11: The Pharisee stood and prayed about himself: God, I thank you that I am not like other people—thieves, wrongdoers, adulterers—or even like that tax collector.
18.12: I fast twice a week and give a tenth of everything I earn.
18.13: But the tax collector stood at a distance and would not even look toward heaven. He beat his chest and cried: God, have mercy on me—I am a sinner!
18.14: I tell you, this man went home right with God—the other did not. For everyone who exalts himself will be brought low, and everyone who humbles himself will be raised up.
18.15: People were bringing babies to him so he might touch them. When the disciples saw this, they rebuked the parents.
18.16: Jesus called the children to himself and said: Let the little children come to me—don't stop them. The kingdom of God belongs to people like these.
18.17: Truly I tell you: whoever does not receive the kingdom of God like a child will never enter it.
18.18: A certain ruler asked him: Good Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?
18.19: Jesus said to him: Why do you call me good? No one is good but God alone.
18.20: You know the commandments: Do not commit adultery. Do not murder. Do not steal. Do not give false testimony. Honor your father and mother.
18.21: All these I have kept from my youth, he said.
18.22: Hearing this, Jesus said to him: One thing you still lack. Sell everything you have and give to the poor—you will have treasure in heaven—and then come, follow me.
18.23: Hearing this, he became deeply sorrowful—for he was very rich.
18.24: Jesus, seeing how sorrowful he had become, said: How hard it is for the wealthy to enter the kingdom of God!
18.25: It is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.
18.26: Those who heard this said: Then who can be saved?
18.27: He said: What is impossible for people is possible with God.
18.28: Peter said: Look—we have left everything we had and followed you.
18.29: He said to them: Truly I tell you—no one who has left home or wife or brothers or parents or children for the kingdom of God's sake
18.30: will fail to receive many times over in this life—and in the age to come, eternal life.
18.31: He took the Twelve aside and said: Look—we are going up to Jerusalem, and everything written through the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled.
18.32: He will be handed over to the Gentiles—mocked, insulted, and spat upon.
18.33: They will flog him and kill him. On the third day he will rise.
18.34: But they understood none of it. The meaning was hidden from them, and they did not grasp what Jesus was saying.
18.35: As he drew near to Jericho, a blind man sat by the road begging.
18.36: Hearing the crowd passing by, he asked what was happening.
18.37: They told him: Jesus of Nazareth is passing by.
18.38: He called out: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!
18.39: Those in front rebuked him, telling him to be quiet. But he cried out all the louder: Son of David, have mercy on me!
18.40: Jesus stopped and commanded that the man be brought to him. When he came close, Jesus asked him,
18.41: What do you want me to do for you? Lord, he said, let me see again.
18.42: Jesus said: Receive your sight—your faith has made you well.
18.43: Instantly he received his sight and followed Jesus, glorifying God—and when all the people saw it, they broke into praise to God.

19.1: Jesus entered Jericho and was making his way through.
19.2: A man named Zacchaeus was there—a chief tax collector, and very wealthy.
19.3: He was eager to get a look at Jesus, but he couldn't see him through the crowd because he was too short.
19.4: So he ran on ahead and climbed a sycamore-fig tree to see Jesus, who was about to pass that way.
19.5: When Jesus reached the spot, he looked up and said: Zacchaeus, come down right away—I need to stay at your house today!
19.6: Zacchaeus scrambled down at once and welcomed Jesus into his home with great joy.
19.7: Everyone who saw this grumbled: He's gone to be the guest of a sinner!
19.8: But Zacchaeus stood up and announced to the Lord: Lord, I'm giving half my possessions to the poor right now. And if I have cheated anyone out of anything, I'll pay them back four times the amount.
19.9: Jesus said to him: Today salvation has come to this household—for this man, too, is a son of Abraham.
19.10: The Son of Man came to seek out and save those who are lost.
19.11: As the crowd was listening, he went on to tell them a parable—for he was near Jerusalem, and people thought the kingdom of God was going to appear immediately.
19.12: He said: A man of noble birth traveled to a distant country to receive a kingdom for himself and then return.
19.13: Before leaving, he called ten of his servants and gave each one a mina. He said: Invest this and put it to work until I return.
19.14: But his subjects hated him and sent a delegation after him to say: We refuse to have this man rule over us.
19.15: When he returned as king, he summoned the servants to whom he had entrusted the money, to find out how much each had earned.
19.16: The first servant came and reported: Master, your mina has earned ten more minas!
19.17: Well done, good servant! the master said. You were faithful in a very small thing—so take charge of ten cities.
19.18: The second servant said: Master, your mina has earned five more minas.
19.19: He answered: You will be over five cities.
19.20: Then another servant came and said: Master, here is your mina—I kept it stored away wrapped in a cloth.
19.21: I was afraid of you, because you are a demanding man—you take what you didn't deposit and harvest what you didn't plant.
19.22: The master answered: I'll judge you by your own words, you useless servant! You knew I was a demanding man—that I take what I didn't deposit and harvest what I didn't plant.
19.23: Then why didn't you at least put my money in the bank? When I came back I could have collected it with interest.
19.24: He said to those standing nearby: Take the mina away from him and give it to the servant who has ten.
19.25: But Master, they said, he already has ten minas!
19.26: He answered: I tell you—to everyone who has, more will be given. But from the one who has nothing, even what little he has will be taken.
19.27: Now as for those enemies of mine who refused to have me as their king—bring them here and execute them in front of me.
19.28: After saying this, Jesus went on ahead, heading up to Jerusalem.
19.29: As he came near Bethphage and Bethany at the hill called the Mount of Olives, he sent two disciples ahead.
19.30: He said: Go into the village ahead. As you enter, you will find a young colt tied up that no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it back.
19.31: If anyone asks why you're untying it, say: The Lord needs it.
19.32: The two went and found everything exactly as he had said.
19.33: As they untied it, its owners asked: Why are you untying the colt?
19.34: They answered: The Lord needs it.
19.35: They brought the colt to Jesus, threw their cloaks over it, and helped Jesus mount.
19.36: As he rode along, people kept spreading their cloaks on the road ahead of him.
19.37: As he reached the place where the road goes down the Mount of Olives, the whole crowd of disciples burst into joyful praise—shouting aloud to God for all the miracles they had witnessed:
19.38: Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven—and glory in the highest!
19.39: Some of the Pharisees in the crowd said to him: Teacher, tell your disciples to be quiet!
19.40: He replied: I tell you—if they kept quiet, the very stones would cry out.
19.41: As he came near Jerusalem and saw the city spread before him, he wept over it
19.42: and said: If only you had recognized today what would bring you peace! But now it is hidden from your sight.
19.43: The days are coming when your enemies will throw up siege works around you, surround you, and close in on you from every direction.
19.44: They will crush you and your children to the ground—not leaving one stone on another—because you did not recognize the moment when God came to you.
19.45: Then he entered the temple courts and began driving out those who were selling there.
19.46: He said: It is written, My house will be a house of prayer—but you have turned it into a den of robbers!
19.47: Each day he taught in the temple courts. The chief priests, the teachers of the law, and the leaders of the people kept looking for a way to kill him.
19.48: But they couldn't find a way to do it, because all the people hung on his every word.

20.1: One of those days, while he was teaching and announcing the good news in the temple courts, the chief priests, teachers of the law, and elders confronted him.
20.2: They demanded: Tell us—by what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you this authority?
20.3: He answered: I have a question for you as well. Tell me—
20.4: Was John's baptism from God, or was it merely human in origin?
20.5: They debated it among themselves: If we say, From God, he'll ask, Then why didn't you believe John?
20.6: But if we say, It was merely human, the people will stone us—because they all believe John was a prophet.
20.7: So they replied that they didn't know.
20.8: Jesus said: Then neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.
20.9: Then he told the people this parable: A man planted a vineyard, leased it to tenant farmers, and went away for a long time.
20.10: At harvest time he sent a servant to collect his share of the fruit. But the tenants beat him and sent him back empty-handed.
20.11: He sent another servant, who was also beaten, humiliated, and sent away empty-handed.
20.12: He sent a third, and they wounded him and threw him out.
20.13: Then the owner of the vineyard said: What should I do? I'll send my beloved son—surely they will respect him.
20.14: But when the tenants saw him, they said to each other: This is the heir—let's kill him, and the inheritance will be ours!
20.15: So they threw him out of the vineyard and killed him. What then will the owner do to those tenants?
20.16: He will come and put those tenants to death and give the vineyard to others. When the crowd heard this, they said: God forbid!
20.17: But he looked at them and said: Then what does this Scripture mean? The stone the builders rejected has become the very cornerstone.
20.18: Anyone who falls on that stone will be shattered—and anyone it falls on will be crushed to powder.
20.19: At that very moment the teachers of the law and the chief priests wanted to arrest him—they knew the parable was aimed at them—but they feared the people.
20.20: So they watched closely and sent spies pretending to be sincere. They hoped to trap him in his words so they could hand him over to the authority of the governor.
20.21: They asked him: Teacher, we know you speak and teach what is true—you don't play favorites but genuinely teach God's ways.
20.22: Is it right for us to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?
20.23: He saw right through their scheming and said:
20.24: Show me a denarius. Whose portrait and inscription are on it? Caesar's, they said.
20.25: Then give to Caesar what belongs to Caesar—and give to God what belongs to God.
20.26: They failed to trap him in his words before the people. Astonished by his answer, they fell silent.
20.27: Some Sadducees—the ones who deny the resurrection—came to Jesus with a question.
20.28: Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man dies leaving a wife but no children, his brother should marry her and have children to carry on his brother's name.
20.29: Now suppose there were seven brothers. The first one married and died without children.
20.31: and then the third married her—and the same thing happened with all seven. They died without leaving children.
20.33: At the resurrection, then, whose wife will she be? All seven had married her.
20.34: Jesus said: The people of this present age marry and are given in marriage.
20.35: But those who are considered worthy to share in the coming age and in the resurrection from the dead will not marry or be given in marriage.
20.36: They cannot die anymore, for they are like the angels—they are children of God because they share in the resurrection.
20.37: But that the dead are raised—Moses himself showed this in the account of the burning bush. He calls the Lord the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.
20.38: He is not the God of the dead but of the living—because to him, all are alive.
20.39: Some of the teachers of the law said: Well answered, teacher!
20.40: After that, no one dared to question him further.
20.41: Then Jesus said to them: How can they say the Messiah is the son of David?
20.42: David himself says in the Book of Psalms: The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand
20.43: until I make your enemies a footstool under your feet.
20.44: David calls him Lord—so how can he be David's son?
20.45: While all the people were listening, he said to his disciples:
20.46: Watch out for the teachers of the law. They love to parade around in flowing robes, enjoy being greeted respectfully in the marketplace, and crave the best seats in the synagogue and the places of honor at banquets.
20.47: They eat up widows' property and offer long prayers just for show. These men will face the severest judgment.

21.1: Looking up, Jesus saw wealthy people dropping their gifts into the temple treasury.
21.2: He also noticed a poor widow drop in two small copper coins.
21.3: He said: I tell you the truth—this poor widow has given more than all the others.
21.4: All the others gave out of their surplus wealth. But she, out of her poverty, put in everything she had to live on.
21.5: Some of his disciples were admiring the temple—its beautiful stones and the offerings decorating it. Jesus said:
21.6: All this you see—days are coming when not one stone will be left on another. Every one will be thrown down.
21.7: Teacher, they asked, when will this happen? And what sign will warn us these things are about to occur?
21.8: He said: Watch out—don't be deceived. Many will come claiming to be me, saying: I am he! or The time is here! Don't follow them.
21.9: When you hear about wars and revolutions, don't be alarmed. These things must happen first, but the end isn't coming yet.
21.10: Then he continued: Nation will go to war against nation, and kingdom against kingdom.
21.11: There will be massive earthquakes, famines, and plagues in various places—terrifying events and great signs from heaven.
21.12: But before all that happens, they will arrest you and persecute you—dragging you before synagogues and throwing you into prison. You will be brought before kings and governors, all because of me.
21.13: This will be your opportunity to testify.
21.14: So make up your mind not to prepare your defense in advance.
21.15: I will give you the words and wisdom that none of your opponents will be able to counter or contradict.
21.16: You will be betrayed even by parents, brothers and sisters, relatives, and friends—and some of you will be put to death.
21.17: Everyone will hate you because of me.
21.18: Yet not a single hair of your head will be lost.
21.19: By standing firm, you will save your lives.
21.20: When you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, you will know that its destruction is near.
21.21: Then those in Judea must flee to the mountains. Those inside the city must get out. And those in the countryside must not go back in.
21.22: For these are days of divine punishment—the fulfillment of everything that is written.
21.23: How terrible it will be for pregnant women and nursing mothers in those days! There will be great suffering throughout the land and God's wrath against this people.
21.24: They will be cut down by the sword and taken as prisoners to every nation. Jerusalem will be trampled under foot by the Gentiles until their era comes to its end.
21.25: Then there will be signs in the sun, moon, and stars. On the earth, the nations will be in despair—bewildered by the roaring and surging sea.
21.26: People will faint in terror, dreading what is coming on the world—for the very powers of heaven will be shaken.
21.27: Then they will see the Son of Man coming on a cloud, with power and great glory.
21.28: When these things begin to happen, stand up tall and lift your heads—because your redemption is getting close.
21.29: He taught them with a parable: Look at the fig tree—and all the trees.
21.30: When they leaf out, you can see for yourselves and know that summer is close.
21.31: In the same way, when you see these things happening, you know that God's kingdom is near.
21.32: I tell you with certainty—this generation will not pass away until all these things have taken place.
21.33: Heaven and earth will disappear—but my words will never disappear.
21.34: But watch yourselves—don't let your hearts get weighed down with indulgence, drinking, and the worries of this life, or that day will spring on you like a trap.
21.35: It will come upon everyone who lives on the face of the earth.
21.36: Stay alert at all times—and pray that you will have the strength to escape everything that is about to happen, and that you will be able to stand before the Son of Man.
21.37: Each day Jesus taught in the temple courts, and each evening he went out to spend the night on the Mount of Olives.
21.38: And all the people came early each morning to hear him at the temple.

22.1: The Festival of Unleavened Bread — called the Passover — was drawing near.
22.2: The chief priests and teachers of the law were searching for a way to kill Jesus, but they were afraid of the people.
22.3: At this point, Satan entered Judas called Iscariot — one of the Twelve.
22.4: Judas went to the chief priests and temple officers to discuss how he could hand Jesus over to them.
22.5: They were glad and offered him money.
22.6: He agreed, and from then on looked for a chance to hand Jesus over when no crowd was around.
22.7: Then came the day of Unleavened Bread, when the Passover lamb had to be slaughtered.
22.8: Jesus sent Peter and John ahead: Go and prepare the Passover meal for us to eat.
22.9: Where do you want us to prepare it? they asked.
22.10: He said: When you enter the city, a man carrying a water jar will meet you. Follow him to the house he enters.
22.11: Tell the owner of the house: The Teacher asks — Where is the guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?
22.12: He will show you a large upper room, already furnished. Make your preparations there.
22.13: They went and found everything exactly as he had said. And they prepared the Passover.
22.14: When the hour arrived, Jesus took his place at the table with his apostles.
22.15: He said to them: I have deeply longed to share this Passover with you before my suffering.
22.16: For I tell you: I will not eat it again until it finds its fulfillment in the kingdom of God.
22.17: He took the cup, gave thanks, and said: Take this and share it among yourselves.
22.18: For I tell you: I will not drink from the fruit of the vine again until the kingdom of God comes.
22.19: He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them, saying: This is my body — given for you. Do this in remembrance of me.
22.20: After supper he took the cup in the same way, saying: This cup is the new covenant — sealed in my blood that is poured out for you.
22.21: But look — the hand of the one who will betray me is here at the table with mine.
22.22: The Son of Man goes as it is written — but woe to that man through whom he is betrayed!
22.23: They began to ask one another which of them it could possibly be who would do this.
22.24: A dispute also broke out among them — which of them should be regarded as the greatest.
22.25: He said to them: Among the nations, kings dominate their subjects, and those who hold authority are called Benefactors.
22.26: But with you it must be different. The greatest among you must become like the youngest, and the leader like the one who serves.
22.27: Which is greater — the one reclining at the table, or the one serving? Surely the one at the table. Yet I am here among you as the one who serves.
22.28: You are the ones who have stood with me through all my trials.
22.29: And I grant you a kingdom, just as my Father granted one to me —
22.30: so that you will eat and drink at my table in my kingdom and sit on thrones governing the twelve tribes of Israel.
22.31: Simon, Simon — Satan has demanded to sift you all like wheat.
22.32: But I have prayed for you, Simon, that your faith would not fail. And when you have come back, strengthen your brothers.
22.33: Peter said: Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death.
22.34: Jesus said: I tell you, Peter — before the rooster crows today, you will deny three times that you know me.
22.35: He asked them: When I sent you out without purse, bag, or sandals, did you lack anything? Nothing, they replied.
22.36: But now, he said, if you have a purse, take it — and a bag. And if you have no sword, sell your cloak and buy one.
22.37: For I tell you: this scripture must be fulfilled in me — He was counted among the lawless. What is written about me is coming to its fulfillment.
22.38: They said: Lord, look — we have two swords here. Enough of this! he said.
22.39: He went out as was his custom to the Mount of Olives, and his disciples followed him.
22.40: When they reached the place, he said to them: Pray that you will not be brought to the test.
22.41: He withdrew about a stone's throw, knelt down, and prayed:
22.42: Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me — yet not my will, but yours be done.
22.43: An angel from heaven appeared to him and gave him strength.
22.44: And in his anguish he prayed even more intensely, and his sweat fell like drops of blood onto the ground.
22.45: When he rose from prayer and returned to his disciples, he found them asleep — worn out from grief.
22.46: Why are you sleeping? he said. Get up and pray, so that you will not be brought to the test.
22.47: While he was still speaking, a crowd arrived — led by Judas, one of the Twelve. He came up to Jesus to kiss him.
22.48: Jesus said to him: Judas — are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss?
22.49: When those around him saw what was about to happen, they asked: Lord, shall we strike with our swords?
22.50: One of them struck the high priest's servant and cut off his right ear.
22.51: But Jesus said: No more of this! He touched the man's ear and healed him.
22.52: Then Jesus said to the chief priests, temple officers, and elders who had come for him: Have you come out with swords and clubs as though I were a revolutionary?
22.53: Day after day I was with you in the temple and you never laid a hand on me. But this is your hour — the hour of darkness.
22.54: They seized him and led him away to the house of the high priest. Peter followed at a distance.
22.55: They kindled a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sat together, and Peter sat among them.
22.56: A servant girl saw him sitting in the firelight, stared at him, and said: This man was with him too.
22.57: He denied it: Woman, I don't know him.
22.58: A little later, someone else noticed him: You are one of them too! Man, I am not! Peter said.
22.59: About an hour later another person insisted: This man really was with him — you can tell by his Galilean accent.
22.60: Peter said: Man, I don't know what you're talking about! At that very moment, as he was still speaking, a rooster crowed.
22.61: The Lord turned and looked directly at Peter. And Peter remembered the Lord's words — Before the rooster crows today, you will deny me three times.
22.62: He went outside and broke down weeping.
22.63: The men guarding Jesus began mocking him and beating him.
22.64: They blindfolded him and demanded: Prophesy! Tell us — who just hit you?
22.65: And they hurled many other insults at him.
22.66: At daybreak the elders of the people — chief priests and teachers of the law — assembled. Jesus was brought before their council.
22.67: If you are the Messiah, tell us! He answered: If I tell you, you will not believe.
22.68: And if I ask you, you will not answer.
22.69: But from this moment on, the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God.
22.70: They all demanded: So you are the Son of God? He said: You are right to say I am.
22.71: They said: What more testimony do we need? We have heard it from his own mouth.

23.1: The entire assembly rose and brought Jesus to Pilate.
23.2: They began their accusations: We found this man stirring up our nation, forbidding us to pay taxes to Caesar, and claiming to be the Messiah — a king.
23.3: Pilate asked him: Are you the king of the Jews? Jesus answered: That is what you say.
23.4: Pilate announced to the chief priests and the crowd: I find no charge against this man.
23.5: But they pressed harder: He is inciting the people with his teaching — all through Judea, from Galilee all the way to here.
23.6: When Pilate heard this, he asked whether Jesus was from Galilee.
23.7: Learning that Jesus was under Herod's authority, he sent him to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem at that time.
23.8: Herod was delighted to see Jesus — he had wanted to meet him for a long time. He had heard about him and was hoping to see him perform some miracle.
23.9: He questioned Jesus at length, but Jesus said nothing.
23.10: The chief priests and teachers of the law stood there, hurling their accusations.
23.11: Then Herod and his soldiers mocked and ridiculed him. They put a splendid robe on him and sent him back to Pilate.
23.12: That very day Herod and Pilate became friends — they had been enemies before.
23.13: Pilate called together the chief priests, the rulers, and the people.
23.14: He told them: You brought me this man as someone who was stirring up the people. I have examined him in your presence and found no basis whatsoever for your charges.
23.15: Nor has Herod — he sent him back to us. As you can see, this man has done nothing deserving death.
23.16: So I will have him flogged and then release him.
23.17: For it was the custom at the feast for him to release one prisoner to the people.
23.18: But they all shouted together: Away with this man! Release Barabbas to us!
23.19: (Barabbas had been imprisoned for starting a rebellion in the city and for murder.)
23.20: Wanting to release Jesus, Pilate appealed to them a second time.
23.21: But they kept shouting: Crucify him! Crucify him!
23.22: A third time he said to them: What crime has this man committed? I found nothing in him deserving death. I will have him flogged and release him.
23.23: But they shouted louder and louder, insisting that he be crucified — and their demands won.
23.24: Pilate gave in and granted their demand.
23.25: He released the man imprisoned for insurrection and murder — the very one they demanded — and handed Jesus over to their will.
23.26: As they led Jesus away, they seized a man named Simon of Cyrene who was coming in from the countryside. They laid the cross on him to carry behind Jesus.
23.27: A great crowd followed, including women who were mourning and wailing for him.
23.28: Jesus turned to them and said: Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me — weep for yourselves and for your children.
23.29: For the days are coming when people will say: How fortunate are the childless women — those who never bore children and never nursed them!
23.30: Then people will cry to the mountains: Fall on us! and to the hills: Cover us!
23.31: For if they do these things when the wood is green — to an innocent man — what will they do when it is dry, and judgment falls on the guilty?
23.32: Two other men — both criminals — were led out to be executed with him.
23.33: When they reached the place called The Skull, they crucified him there — and the two criminals with him, one on his right, one on his left.
23.34: Jesus said: Father, forgive them — they do not know what they are doing. They divided his clothes by casting lots.
23.35: The crowd stood watching. The rulers kept sneering: He saved others — let him save himself, if he really is the Messiah of God, the Chosen One!
23.36: The soldiers also mocked him — coming up and offering him sour wine,
23.37: saying: If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself!
23.38: An inscription above him read: This is the King of the Jews.
23.39: One of the criminals hanging beside him kept hurling abuse: Aren't you the Messiah? Save yourself — and us!
23.40: But the other criminal rebuked him: Do you not fear God? We are under the same condemnation.
23.41: We deserve our sentence — we are getting exactly what our actions earned. But this man has done nothing wrong.
23.42: Then he said: Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.
23.43: Jesus said to him: Truly I tell you — today you will be with me in Paradise.
23.44: It was about noon when darkness fell over the whole land, lasting until three in the afternoon.
23.45: The sun stopped shining. And the curtain of the temple was torn in two.
23.46: Then Jesus cried out with a loud voice: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit! Having said this, he breathed his last.
23.47: When the centurion saw what had happened, he glorified God and said: This man truly was innocent — a righteous man.
23.48: All the crowds who had gathered for this spectacle, when they saw what had happened, went home beating their chests in grief.
23.49: All his acquaintances and the women who had followed him from Galilee stood watching from a distance.
23.50: Now there was a man named Joseph — a member of the Council, a good and righteous man —
23.51: who had not agreed with the Council's decision and action. He was from Arimathea, a town in Judea, and he was himself looking forward to the kingdom of God.
23.52: He went to Pilate and asked for Jesus' body.
23.53: He took the body down, wrapped it in a linen cloth, and placed it in a rock-cut tomb where no one had ever been buried.
23.54: It was Preparation Day, and the Sabbath was beginning.
23.55: The women who had come with Jesus from Galilee followed and saw the tomb and how his body was laid.
23.56: Then they went home and prepared spices and perfumes. They rested on the Sabbath, as the commandment required.

24.1: On the first day of the week, very early in the morning, the women came to the tomb, bringing the spices they had prepared.
24.2: They found the stone rolled away from the entrance.
24.3: When they went inside, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus.
24.4: While they were puzzling over this, two men in brilliantly shining clothes suddenly stood beside them.
24.5: The women were terrified and bowed with their faces to the ground. The men said to them: Why are you looking for the living among the dead?
24.6: He is not here — he has risen! Remember how he told you, while he was still in Galilee:
24.7: The Son of Man must be handed over to sinful men, be crucified, and on the third day rise again.
24.9: They went back from the tomb and told all this to the Eleven and to all the others.
24.10: It was Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the other women with them who brought this report to the apostles.
24.11: But the apostles did not believe them — their words seemed like nonsense.
24.12: Peter got up and ran to the tomb. He bent down and looked in and saw only the linen cloths lying there. He went away, amazed at what had happened.
24.13: That same day two of them were on their way to a village called Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem.
24.14: As they walked, they were discussing all that had taken place.
24.15: As they talked and debated, Jesus himself came and walked alongside them.
24.16: But they were kept from recognizing him.
24.17: He asked them: What are you discussing as you walk along? They stopped, their faces downcast.
24.18: One of them — Cleopas — said: Are you the only person visiting Jerusalem who doesn't know what has happened there these past days?
24.19: What things? he asked. They said: About Jesus of Nazareth — a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people.
24.20: The chief priests and our rulers handed him over to be sentenced to death — and they crucified him.
24.21: We had hoped he was the one who was going to redeem Israel. And on top of all this, it is now the third day since these things happened.
24.22: What is more, some women from our group left us astonished — they went to the tomb early this morning
24.23: and did not find his body. They came back saying they had seen a vision of angels who told them he was alive.
24.24: Some of our companions went to the tomb and found it just as the women had described — but they did not see him.
24.25: He said to them: How slow you are — slow of understanding, slow to believe everything the prophets declared!
24.26: Was it not necessary for the Messiah to suffer these things before entering his glory?
24.27: Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he walked them through the entire Scriptures, explaining everything that pointed to himself.
24.28: As they came near the village they were heading for, he gave the impression he was going on farther.
24.29: But they pressed him urgently: Stay with us! It is nearly evening — the day is almost over. So he went in to stay with them.
24.30: When he sat at the table with them, he took the bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them.
24.31: At that moment their eyes were opened and they recognized him — and he vanished from their sight.
24.32: They said to each other: Were our hearts not burning within us as he talked with us on the road and opened the Scriptures to us?
24.33: They got up that very hour and returned to Jerusalem. They found the Eleven and those with them gathered together,
24.34: saying: It is true! The Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon.
24.35: Then the two of them told what had happened on the road and how they had recognized him in the breaking of the bread.
24.36: While they were still talking about this, Jesus himself appeared among them and said: Peace be with you.
24.37: They were terrified — convinced they were seeing a ghost.
24.38: He said to them: Why are you troubled? Why are doubts rising in your hearts?
24.39: Look at my hands and feet — it really is I! Touch me and see. A ghost has no flesh and bones, as you can see I have.
24.40: Saying this, he showed them his hands and feet.
24.41: While they were still overwhelmed with joy and amazement — too astonished to believe — he asked them: Do you have anything here to eat?
24.42: They gave him a piece of broiled fish.
24.43: He took it and ate it right there in front of them.
24.44: He said to them: This is what I told you while I was still with you — that everything written about me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms had to be fulfilled.
24.45: Then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures.
24.46: He told them: This is what is written — the Messiah must suffer and rise from the dead on the third day,
24.47: and repentance leading to forgiveness of sins must be proclaimed in his name to all nations — starting from Jerusalem.
24.48: You are witnesses of all this.
24.49: I am sending you what my Father has promised. But stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high.
24.50: He led them out to the vicinity of Bethany. There he lifted up his hands and blessed them.
24.51: While he was blessing them, he parted from them and was taken up into heaven.
24.52: They worshipped him, then returned to Jerusalem with great joy.
24.53: And they were continually in the temple, praising God.
